Original comment by: Jason R. Coombs

Thank you for the reply.  It helped me a lot, but I've come up with another problem, mainly because I'm using Python 3.

The library has somewhat mixed uses between `bytes` and `str`, and when you convert `bytes` to `str` implicitly it would result `"b'this'"`.

We should explicitly choose what to use between two kinds of strings, and I would like to recommend `bytes`.  For example, the channel names are allowed to contain almost any sequences of bytes as specified by RFC 1459, so `bytes` should be suitable.  But when you do that, every line would become problematic:

 - In `irc.client.is_channel()`: `string[0] in "#&+!"`
 - In `irc.client.ServerConnection.join()`: `"JOIN %s%s" % (channel, (key and (" " + key)))`
 - `NickMask(prefix)` when a privmsg event has occured

So I'm trying to convert all the internal strings to `bytes` on my fork, in a similar fashion I've done to `irclib`: https://github.com/puzzlet/python-irclib

Note that you won't see the downloaded package because it's not downloaded by `pip` as a dependency, it's actually downloaded by `setuptools` when `egg_info` is performed by pip to install it. Also it's downloaded in `.eggs` so it's not visibile by default.

You will notice that if you run:

```
$ pip install sshuttle -v --no-cache-dir --no-clean --no-binary :all:
```

And you stop it (`CTRL+Z`) right after ` Running setup.py (path:/tmp/pip-build-Gzd6Su/sshuttle/setup.py) egg_info for package sshuttle` phrase (I'm not aware of any way to prevent pip from deleting the packages build dir after it installs them) you will find `setuptools_scm` in `/tmp/pip-build-Gzd6Su/sshuttle/.eggs` which means that `setuptools` downloaded the package through `easy_install` and made it available to the setup phase.

99% of the times this won't be an issue, especially since people will install `wheels`, but I'm not sure it makes sense to face the 1% issues just to avoid the effort of manually updating the version :D 

Kajiki for example achieves a similar result through https://github.com/nandoflorestan/kajiki/blob/master/release_new_version.py which takes care of tagging the repository and setting the version automatically without the need to extend the setup script.

OK, so now back to your pull request:

1. @signalled is a bad idea, as not every call to the setter will change the value (and therefore should emit the signal; for example, we don't want to emit if the new value is equal to the old value); and not always the value is changed by a call to the setter (for example if we're observing some remote / hardware variable). Normal @PropertyName.setter + emitting the PropertyChanged signal manually is a better way here. (pydbus in the future might provide its own property() that's automatically handling getting, setting and emiting the signal; but that's outside of the scope of this feature)

2. @dbus_method/@dbus_property should be in pydbus.strong_typing module, and should be called @typed_method((arg,...), ret), @typed_property(t) and typed_signal(arg,...). You don't have to implement type checking, I can implement that later.

3. gen_introspection should live in pydbus.xml_generator module and should be called generate_introspection_xml(require_strong_typing=False); it should be imported into the main pydbus module. It should generate XML introspection for all members that start with an uppercase letter and are not in ("PropertiesChanged", "Get", "GetAll", "Set"). For the strongly typed ones, it should output types to the XML; for untyped ones it should use "TODO" instead

4. @dbus_interface should also live in pydbus.xml_generator module and be imported into main pydbus module. I'm not sure how it should be called, as I don't want people to think it's the recommended way to provide XML introspection; but on the other hand, it shouldn't be too complicated. I lean toward @autogenerate_introspection_xml(interface_name).

Note that it should work in the same way as
```
class xxx(object):
    ...

xxx.dbus = generate_introspection_xml(xxx, require_strong_typing = True)
```

5. It would be awesome if you could put the strong_typing-related things in one commit, and generate_introspection_xml-related things in a second one.

Quick comment... try gmpy2,mpc(x.real, x.imag, precision=1) == x.

Specifying a precision of 1 should do an exact conversion if x.real and
y.real are either binary floating point (Python float or mpfr) or an
integer. At least some of the internal code paths do that. Since MPFR 4 now
considers 1 a valid precision, I want to change that magic value to -1.

It is possible to make the default value of precision be -1 instead of the
value in current context. This will allow lossless conversion by default.
You would need to specify a precision (or possibly pass a context) to get a
value converted to a different context. I personally like this option but
it is a backwards-incompatible change.

One thing that I feel has been lost in much of the discussion about Curio is the important of environmental isolation.   In any non-trivial application, you are going to have synchronous code, asynchronous code, and possibly synchronous code that involves threads.  This presents a certain challenge for API design.   For example, consider an event and the process of setting it:

    evt = Event()
    ...
    evt.set()

Is that set() operation safe to use in normal synchronous code?  Does it work in an async environment?  Is it thread-safe?   It almost certainly depends on the kind of Event that is created.  For example, `threading`, `asyncio`, `multiprocessing`, `trio`, and `curio` all have their own implementation of `Event`.    However, only the Curio version requires the use of an `await`.  So, it's this:

    evt = curio.Event()
    ...
    await evt.set()

In all of the quibbling about cancellation points and scheduling, something about isolation got lost.  The reason this is an `await` in Curio is that Events are only safe to use in an async context. It is not safe to use `set()` on a Curio Event in normal synchronous code. It is not safe to use `set()` in threaded code.  The fact that it requires an `await()` makes this explicitly clear---there is no way to even invoke such a function outside of an async environment.   Since that whole possibility is cut off, it makes it much easier to reason about in general. It also makes the implementation of Curio much easier to deal with because it does not have to concern itself with a bunch of esoteric corner cases (i.e., what happens if this gets called by a thread?).   To me, reasoning about matters such as cancellation points are trivial--those are easy to address in Curio and easy to change.  Reasoning about execution environments, however, is a much more difficult problem.  And it's not something which can be easily dismissed in my opinion. 
 
The focus on environments in Curio has opened up a variety of interesting programming avenues as well.  For example, I think Curio `UniversalQueue` objects are pretty interesting.  Asynchronous threads are pretty interesting too.   Or, if you want to blow your mind the `examples/requestio` example in which `requests` runs on top of Curio without modification.  All of these rely pretty heavily on environment isolation.   So, in thinking about APIs, I would hope that this would be a major topic of discussion.

From the traceback, it's obvious that problem is that ``__init__.py`` imports ``icmp.py`` which imports ``ip.py`` which imports ``icmp.py`` during construction of ``dpkt.ip.IP._protosw`` dict (== circular import).

When ``icmp.py`` imports ``ip.py`` that module is not imported yet (it's listed later then ``icmp.py`` in the ``__init__.py``), thus first (real) import of ``ip.py`` is triggered from inside of ``icmp.py`` which means that ``icmp.py`` which is needed inside ``ip.py`` isn't fully loaded yet and thus ``ICMP`` class is not available for the ``ip.py`` module => The class cannot be added into ``dpkt.ip.IP._protosw`` => ICMP protocol is not supported in IP packets.

This issue could be simply avoided by removal of import of ``ip`` module from the beginning of ``icmp.py`` and usage of the ``ip`` module there like ``dpkt.ip.IP()`` instead of ``ip.IP()``:

    diff --git a/dpkt/icmp.py b/dpkt/icmp.py
    index 214a950..54411e1 100644
    --- a/dpkt/icmp.py

I investigated this problem just now. @saylenty, I think using `Decimal` module is a good idea. Since the variable `ts` is of the type of `float`. Thus directly use `Decimal` won't work. Please see the following code.

``` python
>>> from decimal import *
>>> ts=123234345.667883
>>> int((Decimal(ts) - int(ts)) * Decimal('1000000.0'))
667882
```

Thus we need to explicitly set the precision as follows.

``` python
>>> getcontext().prec = 6
>>> int((Decimal(ts) - int(ts)) * Decimal('1000000.0'))
667883
```

In my opinion, using `round` directly would be a better choice. I've sent a PR on this issue.

Thank you for your interest in using *isbnlib*.

Answering your questions:

**first, what would be the procedure?**
The procedure is more or less what you have done... but you are making some mistakes. In your program, you must have `import wcat2`  (I suppose you follow the pattern of `wcat` and that your file with the new provider is `wcat2.py`), then after importing `isbnlib` you must register your service `isbnlib.registry.add_service('wcat2', wcat2.query)`. In `wcat2.py` you must add the new fields to the SERVICE_URL, so `SERVICE_URL=...&fl=...,lang,form,ed` together with what you have already done.                           through.

index 2ad9750..cfc110e 100644
--- a/green/output.py
+++ b/green/output.py
@@ -157,6 +157,13 @@ class GreenStream(object):
             text = text_type(unidecode(text))
         self.stream.write(text)

+    def writelines(self, lines):
+        """
+        Just for better compatibility with real file objects
+        """
+        for line in lines:

     def formatText(self, text, indent=0, outcome_char=''):

import gffutils

with open('tmp.gff', 'w') as fout:

        if feature.featuretype == 'CDS':

            # If level=1 not specified, it will also return gene "grandparent"
            # which you don't care about
            parent = list(db.parents(feature, level=1))

            # sanity checking
            assert len(parent) == 1
            parent = parent[0]

            feature.attributes['Name'] = parent.attributes['Name']
        fout.write(str(feature) + '\n')
```
The order of the attributes differs, even with `create_db(keep_order=True)`, because the original input file doesn't have consistent attribute order itself (the mRNA has pacid before Parent). But as long as you're OK with that, this should work.

Another way of thinking about it is that `update` does this:
```
for i in x:
   # do something with i
```
and it expects `i` to be a `Feature`.  In this case, the [docs](https://docs.python.org/3/glossary.html#term-iterable) you linked to suggests that `x` should be considered an iterable.

In the case of a list, using `for` actually converts the list (an iterable) into an iterator (something that has `__next__` method). But `x` could already be an iterator that has a `__next__` method . . . like the generator in your example.

Fundamentally, installation is indeed really required for releases only, so I do like your suggestion to install only in release mode (I can not say much about the Windows side of things).

For debug scenarios, manually downloading ``dlib`` sounds like a reasonable option to me.

To summarize:
- ``release`` mode installation and use by external projects:
```sh
$ cd $DLIB
$ mkdir build && cd build
$ cmake ../dlib
$ make && make install
```
In external projects:
```cmake
find_package(dlib REQUIRED) #possibly supporting components!
message(STATUS "Using dlib-${dlib_VERSION}")
include_directories(${dlib_INCLUDE_DIRS})
#...
target_link_libraries(my_target ${dlib_LIBRARIES})
```


- ``debug`` mode installation and use by external projects:
```sh
$ # just extract dlib to some place
```
In external projects:
```cmake
set(DLIB_PATH "" CACHE PATH "Path to DLIB")
include(${DLIB_PATH}/dlib/cmake)

# no need for include_directories()
target_link_libraries(my_target dlib)
```

Besides, in ``release`` mode, we configure (and install) ``config.h`` to disable asserts.

Advantages:
- 100% compatible with the current compilation steps. Nothing to change there.
- address the concerns with having a proper installation workflow + ``cmake`` workflow

Drawback:
- no transparent way to change **from** release to **debug** _when using the installed dlib_

I sort of view it as the caller's responsibility and right to determine what error results in terminating the application. In order to ignore your error, the user must explicitly ignore the thrown exception. At this point it's the user's fault for shooting themselves in the foot. By all means, that's their choice. You shouldn't help them debug their code.

Comparing to the OS is a little different as the OS will do whatever it can to keep your application running. If you reach outside your bounds, that's more of an encroachment on other application's space and memory. It's also a security risk. I don't think the comparison is valid. Besides, the comparison would be more like saying if I segfault, the entire machine should shut down, not just the offending application. That makes me feel like it's 1990 again.

In my particular use case, I'm interfacing with a user in an application where only one small sub-task is to do non-linear optimization. If, they accidentally select values that throw this error, I'm happy to report up to them that they are stupid and should choose better numbers. I'm not ignoring the error, I'm elevating to the place where action can be taken to correct it (i.e. in this case, the user) and that's exactly what exceptions are designed to do, elevate the error to the point where action can be taken to correct the error. Only sometimes should that mean termination, and that's the user of the lib's decision, not the lib itself. It should be my choice as the user of the library to decide if the program should exit or not. Should they user again choose to use bad values, exiting the application with no stack unwinding or state saving is simply bad design, and arrogant to assume understanding of the entire state of the system by a single low-level optimization library.

Again, I'm not ignoring the error, I'm providing a proper method for recovery from it, but I just can't do that 2 times in a row...? Why is it ok for a single handling of the exception, but not a second... i.e. why not just call exit the first time??? If the calling code was wrong once, what's to assume that it will not be called again. Why use exceptions at all, just assert right at all the errors (man, feels like 1990 again).

My only choice now is to 1) dynamically load / unload the lib to reset the local static, or 2) provide redundant checking of the user's input, which you check anyway, or 3) use a different non-linear optimization lib, but quite frankly, this one is quite nice except for this small design choice.

What about at least making this kill-my-program-after-an-easily-recoverable-error thing a configurable option -- wait, that's what exceptions are, my choice to ignore errors and (potentially) screw myself in the process.

Seems like a pretty reasonable pull request to me...

> Hi Pascal, I am using your great package on a data science project Great! Thanks for the feedback. At the moment, there is no canned way to add a legend to a symbol layer (this would make a great addition, PRs welcome!). You are probably best off drawing your own using jupyter widgets. Since gmaps maps are themselves Jupyter widgets, you can use them as building blocks for more complex visualisations. For instance: ```py import random from matplotlib.cm import tab10 # Use the matplotlib tab10 colormap from matplotlib.colors import to_hex import ipywidgets as widgets gmaps.configure(api_key='...') def gen_random_locations(npoints): """ Generate `npoints` random locations in the US """ latitudes = [random.uniform(32.7, 48.6) for _ in range(npoints)] longitudes = [random.uniform(-124.5, -77.0) for _ in range(npoints)] return list(zip(latitudes, longitudes)) # These are the 10 years that make up your data years = [1997, 1998, 1999, 2000, 2001, 2002, 2003, 2004, 2005, 2006] assert len(years) == 10 data = {year: gen_random_locations(10) for year in years} colors = {year: to_hex(tab10(iyear)) for iyear, year in enumerate(years)} # generate a colour for each year # Some CSS hackery to draw a circle of that color next to the year legend_template = '● {year}' def draw_map_with_legend(): figure = gmaps.figure() legend_entries = [] for iyear, year in enumerate(years): locations = data[year] color = colors[year] points_layer = gmaps.symbol_layer( locations, fill_color=color, stroke_color=color, fill_opacity=0.7, stroke_opacity=0.7 ) figure.add_layer(points_layer) legend_entries.append(legend_template.format(color=color, year=year)) legend = widgets.HBox( [widgets.HTML(legend_entry) for legend_entry in legend_entries], layout=widgets.Layout(width='100%', justify_content='space-between') ) return widgets.VBox([figure, legend]) draw_map_with_legend() ``` Note the following: - we use the [HBox](https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/latest/examples/Widget%20List.html#HBox) and [VBox](https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/latest/examples/Widget%20List.html#VBox) widgets to stack widgets (like maps and legend entries). - we use a matplotlib colormap to get some colors that go well together. I chose the `tab10` at random. See [the matplotlib docs](https://matplotlib.org/examples/color/colormaps_reference.html) for other color maps. You can also just choose arbitrary colors, obviously.

from the help:

```
[…]
    git-repo [--path=<path>] [-v...] <target> create [--add]
    git-repo [--path=<path>] [-v...] <target> create <user>/<repo> [--add]
[…]
Options for create:
    --add                    Add to local repository after creation
[…]
```

i.e.: if you do:

```
git lab create --add group/repo
```

it will be added to the current repository, if you're in one.

I decided to default to not change the local directory per default, because you're not necessarily creating a repository when you're in a directory you've already `git init`ed. And removing a remote is not hard, but because names are autogenerated it's not always obvious (`--add` will create two remotes, `gitlab` and `all`). 

But, if you forgot to add, it's really not a big deal, you just do one more command:

```
git lab add group/repo gitlab
```

cf the `-h` for add:

```
    git-repo [--path=<path>] [-v...] <target> add <user>/<repo> [<name>] [--tracking=<branch>] [-a]
[…]
Options for add:
    <name>                   Name to use for the remote (defaults to name of repo)
    -t,--tracking=<branch>   Makes this remote tracking for the current branch
    -a,--alone               Does not add the remote to the 'all' remote
```

I'm planning to split the huge `--help` screen into different `--help` for each subcommand, but at the time being, having them all in one place makes it easier to mold the CLI API.

Any thoughts?

full example
```python
import sys
import itertools
from PyQt5.QtWidgets import QApplication, QMessageBox
#from PyQt5.QtCore import
import asyncio
import quamash 

def dialog_async_exec(dialog):
    future = asyncio.Future()
    dialog.finished.connect(lambda r: future.set_result(r))
    dialog.open()
    return future

@asyncio.coroutine
def main():
    global app
    dialog = QMessageBox(QMessageBox.Question,
                         "Application Name",
                         "Hardware failure.\n\nDisk error detected\nDo you want to stop?")

    dialog.addButton(QMessageBox.Yes)
    dialog.setDefaultButton(QMessageBox.Yes)
    dialog.addButton(QMessageBox.No)
    dialog.setEscapeButton(QMessageBox.No)
    result = yield from dialog_async_exec(dialog)
    if result == QMessageBox.Yes:
        print('Yes!')
    elif result == QMessageBox.No:
        print('No!')
    else:
        print('Something else')

@asyncio.coroutine
def weirdloop():
    # this keeps looping to prove the loop is still running
    for i in itertools.count():
        print('loop count', i)
        yield from asyncio.sleep(1)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app = QApplication(sys.argv)
    loop = quamash.QEventLoop(app)
    asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)
    asyncio.async(weirdloop())
    with loop:
        loop.run_until_complete(main())
```

stdout_version, version = p.communicate()
version = _read_string(version).strip()
if version == '':
    version = stdout_version
```

One more problem that actually matters:

We can't have display tests conform to pep8 because you need to set `matplotlib.use('Agg')` before importing `matplotlib.pyplot`.  pep8 requires all package imports to be grouped at the top, so you can't do the following in `test_display.py`:

```python
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use('Agg')
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
...
```
The following does conform to pep8, but doesn't actually work:
```python
import matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
matplotlib.use('Agg')
...
```
TLDR: we can have pep8, or display tests, but not both.

It seems the same as **[issue #20](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/issues/20)**. From my experience, it happens once in a while, and is quite normal.

This is because Telegram servers sometimes take too long to respond to the `getUpdates` request. If the message loop keeps running (that is, you can still receive messages), there is nothing to worry about.

Regarding whether the Session object is thread-safe ...

The [issue and comments](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/1871) that you referred to was from 2 years ago. And a lot of recent issues still question `requests`'s thread-safety:

- [Document threading contract for Session class](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/2766)
- [request.Session not thread-safe](https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/2377)
- [Possible thread-unsafety due to requests.Session](https://github.com/fprimex/zdesk/issues/29)

Not reassuring, to say the least. **Please don't try to convince me under what conditions it might be thread-safe or not, or how it "seems ok in practice". On thread-safety, I don't take chances.**

The most consistent advice the author can give is to use "one session per thread". **I am not going to do one session per thread** in telepot. We are talking to the same host all the time; all threads should share the same connection pool.

This investigation led me to `urllib3`, which is more lightweight, has connection pool, and has a thread-safety guarantee from the author. I am going to move to `urllib3` for all HTTP requests, except for downloading files. Because streaming download is more easily done in `requests`, I will keep that part using `requests`. For all other HTTP connections, I am going to use `urllib3`.

For telepot's async version, I am also going to incorporate connection pool. That part is easier because of no thread-safety concerns.

Expect connection pooling along with routing features on or before **May 17<sup>th</sup>**, if no unexpected obstacle occurs.

---

However, I'm not sure whether that is technically feasible if we still use some of the CC-BY-SA corpora. For example, if we use Wikipedia corpus, their license has it that any derived work has to be licensed by the same CC-BY-SA license (under the "SA" share-alike cause).  

I have seen another open source project that creates word vectors out of CC-BY-SA corpora. Their project even combines inputs from many sources (sources are mixed with completely free license or CC-BY-SA) to come up with the final model. Their project has two licenses, one for the code and the other for the model data (the final word vectors). They kept the code very open like MIT license, and the word vectors license is bounded to the requirements of the licenses of the original corpora used for training, which is CC-BY-SA. I'm just thinking the situation is similar to what we have here.

    8.7.3.1 To encode an octetstring value in this way, it is segmented.
    Each segment shall consist of a series of consecutive octets of the value.
    There shall be no significance placed on the segment boundaries."

My understanding is that a constructed OctetString still represents a single OctetString value. Imho asn1crypto should return the value as whole,  i.e. without the internal structure, when using OctetString.native or bytes(OctetString).

This can happen when you include a large logo on the title page (as in the Lizmap documentation for example). The space between the title page elements is fixed at the moment. You can override them by specifying a custom style sheet in conf.py:

```
	from rinoh.style import StyleSheetFile

	rinoh_stylesheet = StyleSheetFile('lizmap.rts')
```

And lizmap.rts::

```
    [STYLESHEET]
    name=LizMap custom style
    description=Small tweaks made to the Sphinx style sheet
    base=sphinx

    [title page author]
    typeface=$(sans_typeface)
    font_weight=bold
    font_size=17pt
    text_align=right
    space_above=20pt

    [title page date]
    typeface=$(serif_typeface)
    font_size=12pt
    text_align=right
    space_above=20pt
```

These are copies of the styles defined in sphinx.rts (included with rinohtype), replacing space_above with smaller values. In a future release, it will be possible to inherit from a style, so you don't have to copy the unchanged values.

It would of course be nice if this could be handled automatically, depending on the size of the logo, but this is not a high priority feature IMO.

Upon investigating this, I think this is what the root of the problem is: the new parameter array code uses SQDescribeParam to get BufferLength (among other things), and for the SQL "text" the BufferLength is always 0x7FFFFFFF and since the code is dealing with SQLWCHARs it multiplies it by two to get 
0xFFFFFFFE, which is -2 in 32 bit. The tests fail on a check that is easy enough to fix by casting the BufferLength to unsigned long, but the real problem is SQLBindParam which will fail due to the buffer being set to -2.

I'm not sure what is the best solution to this, either we need to handle "text" types specially and set the buffer to zero when binding them or some generic handling of BufferLength before calling SQLBindParam to make sure it's not negative.

@powahftw Character formatting (font characteristics) are specified at the `Run` level. A `Paragraph` object contains one or more (usually more) runs. When assigning to `Paragraph.text`, all the runs in the paragraph are replaced with a single *new* run. This is why the text formatting disappears; because the runs that contained that formatting disappear.

Although it would not work for all cases one might want, a useful behavior would be to replace the text in a paragraph, retaining the formatting present *in the first run*. This could be accomplished like this:

```python
def replace_paragraph_text_retaining_initial_formatting(paragraph, new_text):
    p = paragraph._p  # the lxml element containing the `<a:p>` paragraph element
    # remove all but the first run
    for idx, run in enumerate(paragraph.runs):
        if idx == 0:
            continue
        p.remove(run._r)
    paragraph.runs[0].text = new_text

paragraph = textframe.paragraph[0]  # or wherever you get the paragraph from
new_text = 'foobar'
replace_paragraph_text_retaining_initial_formatting(paragraph, new_text)
```

I haven't tested this, maybe you can report back any mistakes if you try it out, but I think it gives the gist.

This would be roughly how such a feature would be implemented.

The issue is due to the existence of `ModelBase.metadata = None` in [sqlservice/model.py#L44](https://github.com/dgilland/sqlservice/blob/ac0dad2bfea9b65c01013120feba06fe4ec06068/sqlservice/model.py#L44) which causes [sqlalchemy_utils/functions/orm.py:get_declarative_base](https://github.com/kvesteri/sqlalchemy-utils/blob/40fd44c2bb38a2b0e2c007895483a88a4ecc336c/sqlalchemy_utils/functions/orm.py#L761) (used in [sqlalchemy_continuum/builder.py](https://github.com/kvesteri/sqlalchemy-continuum/blob/ee857beaad6c5ea113fcda31a35c4e11116e70d7/sqlalchemy_continuum/builder.py#L140)) to return the wrong class. The problem is that it sees a `metadata` attribute on `ModelBase` and goes "up" to the next base class which is `object` (i.e. instead of stopping at `ModelBase` it  goes too far and returns `object` which later causes the `has no attribute '_decl_class_registry'` error you received).

The short-term workaround would be to delete the `ModelBase.metadata` attribute as in:

```python
del ModelBase.metadata

@declarative_base
class Model(ModelBase):
    pass
```

Obviously not ideal but it "works" for this case.

Long-term, I need to decide whether to either maintain the status-quo or rename/drop `ModelBase.metadata` (along with `ModelBase.metaclass`) so that there is no conflict (likely a rename). I may lean towards a single "config" class attribute to lessen the impact of this kind of thing and to reduce the special class attributes defined in `ModelBase` (currently there's `ModelBase.metadata`, `metaclass`, and `__dict_args__` so may move all those under a single class attribute instead).

I found similar situation in one of the plc's that I use. First of all according to your document, M2002 doesn't exist. If you look at your discrete status area memory allocation table you will see that it starts at X(256), Y(256), T(256), C(256), TR(40), S(1000) and then M(2002). That means that first register 0000 is X000.
Alternatively M block registers would be addressed as: 
X(256) + Y(256) + T(256) + C(256) + TR(40) + S(1000) - 1(index starts at 0) = 2063
From that we can assume that first M0 relay is at address 2064.
Try read_bit(2064, 1) to read status of M0.

1 - READ discreet output coils

3 - READ analog holding registers
4 - READ analog input registers

**Gathering implementation thoughts:**

This is going to be kind of complex and might take a couple prototypes to get right. 

Probably ties with https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/75 to some extent. 

Simplest approach: 

* Each canonical test gets a clone with dynamic fields substituted, just like [calling the current realize method[(https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/pyresttest/tests.py#L241)
  - Easy to implement I think (create properties for each item, with getters that use an internally bound context at runtime to generate result, then do a lookup for each field this way) 
  - Ex: when parsing with a body from generator, a property is created instead of setting the value.  Property does string.template(_body).safe_substitute(context.get_variables()) or similar. 
  - Bad for benchmarks because they would require creating a copy every time.  But for these if we move the execution into the benchmark class itself, it can do some internal optimization here!
* Full-dynamic property access, using bound context:
  - Canonical test/benchmark steps are copied for runtime use and reused - the context is bound in, and properties are used similar to above, however there is no full 'realize' step applied ahead of time.  Each property access dynamically evaluates, and benchmark may be called with the same context multiple times.

Harder approach:
* Try to anticipate the sequence of values, I.E. pre-generate generator results?

If I'm reading your changes correctly, you've created a validation mode that isn't described in the standards. Point in time validation requires valid revocation data from that point in time, not some future time where the revocation status may have changed. The obvious case is where a certificate in the path expired and the revocation entry was removed from the CRL. A less obvious case is the signer could have been on hold at the time of signing and subsequently removed from hold. My take is if you are generating signatures that need to be point in time validated in the future, those signatures should carry the revocation data with them. Acrobat is an example of an app that does this.

I did some more digging. 

`schema.fields` is a dictionary defining the fields and how to validate them according to marshmallows rules.

`schema` is similar to` schema.fields`, but it indicates that an object should be created not a dictionary. As defined by `Schema.Meta.model`. 

If you pass in `schema.fields`, webargs generates a validated dictionary, which can be iterated over to update kwargs (line 41 in wrapper.py)

If you pass in `schema`, webargs generates an object, which can NOT be iterated over to update kwargs. 

Knowing that, we just need to force apispec and webargs to use fields and not the model-based schema. The simple/easy solution was to send in the marshmallow schema fields to flask_apispec.use_kwargs.

```
    @use_kwargs(StoreSchema().fields)
    def put(self, store_id, **kwargs):
        store = Store.query.get_or_404(store_id)
        store.__dict__.update(**kwargs)
        db.session.commit()
        return store_schema.dump(store).data
```

TLDR; use `StoreSchema().fields` instead of `StoreSchema`

Because this is how class and instance attributes are looked up in Python. The proxy is just proxy, that delegates all method calls to the proxied object, you can't magically replace the `self` object of the original instance. You would need to properly subclass the original class and create the objects yourselves for that.

What you are trying to accomplish is therefore impossible to do with proxies, such as `wrapt.ObjectProxy`. I'm also wondering, if it is even a good idea to do what you want to do in the first place, but I'm missing context to judge that.

What you want to do, has to be done via monkey patching. You could monkey patch the `inner` method of just an instance of the original class or the whole class depending on the scope of your intended changes. With the `unittest.mock.patch` decorator/context manager, you can restrict the monkey patching to the runtime of a given decorated function or a lexical block with the `with` and the original behavior is automatically restored afterwards. If you want to permanently patch an instance/class you simply assign the values yourselves:

```python
class C(object):
    def outer(self):
        return self.inner()

    def inner(self):
        return 7

original_inner = C.inner
def fixed_inner(self):
    return original_inner(self) + 2

# Pristine behavior
original = C()
print(original.inner()) # 7
print(original.outer()) # 7

import unittest.mock

# Temporary patching a single instance
with unittest.mock.patch.object(original, 'inner', fixed_inner.__get__(original, type(original))):
    print(original.inner()) # 9
    print(original.outer()) # 9
    print(C().inner()) # 7
    print(C().outer()) # 7

# Same as above, but with a decorator
@unittest.mock.patch.object(original, 'inner', fixed_inner.__get__(original, type(original))):
def foo():
    print(original.inner()) # 9
    print(original.outer()) # 9
    print(C().inner()) # 7
    print(C().outer()) # 7

foo()

# Temporary patching the whole class
with unittest.mock.patch.object(C, 'inner', fixed_inner):
    print(original.inner()) # 9
    print(original.outer()) # 9
    print(C().inner()) # 9
    print(C().outer()) # 9

# Patching the instance permanently
original.inner = fixed_inner.__get__(original, type(original))
print(original.inner()) # 9
print(original.outer()) # 9
print(C().inner()) # 7
print(C().outer()) # 7

# Undoing it
del original.inner

# Patching the class permanently
C.inner = fixed_inner
print(original.inner()) # 9
print(original.outer()) # 9
print(C().inner()) # 9
print(C().outer()) # 9
```

We have to do some unholy things to make some of the examples above work:
* You might ask, what this `__get__` thing is about. Methods in Python are bound to instances through the Descriptor Protocol, which is only used for class attributes and not for instance attributes, so we have to do bind the `fixed_inner`  to the specific instance.
* We have to retain a reference to the original `inner` method in the `original_inner` variable or we have infinite recursion.

Hello Casper,

1. the vocabulary in the corpus is typically the number of characters plus the number of BPE merge operations (with a few exceptions, especially if you learn BPE on the joint vocabulary). So yes, it is normal that the vocabulary size exceeds the number of BPE symbols. In our experiments, we set the network vocabulary to be slightly larger than the number of BPE operations (60000 and 59500, respectively).

2. we extract the vocabulary from the encoded text, like we do for the unsegmented text. Again, this is because the BPE model does not contain entries for characters, which should also be part of the network vocabulary.

best wishes,
Rico

Actually I'm also considering a class that holds both objective function and constraints, but it might seem a bit overkill for that (in addition to what you said). Yes, maybe we can start of with that.

I'm just wondering why we need to separate both equality and inequality constraints? Hmmm, I'm not sure if this is generally difficult for other Python users (so I've heard), but in my functional programming experience, I found lambda expressions as something very helpful to define expressions. 

So if we have h(x) = 0 and g(x) <= 0 as constraints, I can define:

```python
h = lambda x: x == 0
g = lambda x: x <= 0
```

and so I can evaluate a number or vector,

```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> input = np.array([0,1,2,3])
>>> h(input)
array([True, False, False, False], dtype=bool)
>>> g(input)
array([True, False, False, False], dtype=bool)
```

And thus for my input, we can just lump all of them together in a tuple:

```python
# Define optimizer
optimizer = DebPSO(n_particles=100, dims=10, options=options)
# Run optimization
optimizer.run(n_iters=1e3, objective_func=f, constraints=(h,g))
```

This particular implementation is not incremental and too slow for realtime video (as mentioned in the readme, there are examples where it takes tens of seconds to parse the document from the picture, because it tries several varkous rescalings and rotations searching for OCR-able text in a somewhat brute-force manner).

For realtime you'd need to get rid of this brute force step and find a method to recover a well-aligned MRZ from the image in one pass. One possibility is to train an object tracking model (e.g. something convnet-based), however for that you need training data (and ample time for experimentation).

Even then it might not be perfectly realtime, because the tesseract invocation probably usually takes more than 40ms, to overcome that you'd either need to somehow cache previously computed values or come up with an "incremental OCR", which is a bit of a research project of its own.

the stSearch directive is using a normal search. That means if you use the "Global search" it will search for the text "acc 33" in any of the column. Therefore for the Smart-Table the given text is nowhere to be found.
What you want here is that the "Global search" split the text at spaces and search in every column with every item from the splited text.
This kind of search is something that you need to do on your own. You can extend the stSearch directive or create a new one for this purpose.

But just a info you can create a column based search for every column
You don't have to bring any function or javascript, just use their template and make the plumbing with smart-table API, see [plunker](http://plnkr.co/edit/e82rdx7R4pjbLvayUHXx?p=preview)

This is a problem. However, that's how the django deserializers work.

In general, certain database migrations will just plain break your version history. Certain field type conversions and field deletions are both bad for the version history.

The way around this would be to write a brand new model serialiation framework that's less opaque, and to integrate a series of hooks into the south migration code. This is not an easy task, and would easily double or triple the reversion codebase.

Also, if you want the automated testing @gkovacs then you'll need to enable Travis here: https://travis-ci.org/gkovacs/lz-string-python (then you'll get e-mails telling you if any changes in commits / PRs break the tests on any versions of Python).

Sorry, I've been a bit busy.

My main reason for delaying on this is that I personally have no experience with multi-db django stuff, so I've got no real way of knowing whether the changes you're suggesting are the right way to do things. I'd rather not pull in code that might later have to be re-vamped and break backwards compatibility.

I'm wondering whether a better approach may be to make it so that the DB for the revision is specified in the same way as the comment and the user meta properties. That is, you would call reversion.set_db(db) to set it at any time in the revision stack. That would make it more obvious that it's shared for the whole revision stack, and follow existing paradigms. The current method of deciding the DB when you push onto the revision stack feels like it may lead to a misunderstanding about how it works. (Although your current method of throwing an exception is a good solution too, admittedly, it's not the paradigm that's been established.)

What do you think?

Again, sorry for the delay and wibbling. I have to be pretty careful nowadays in getting things right first time with reversion, since it's got a fair few users now. :)

hashes are not supposed to be unique, but for quickly assigning first buckets in e.g. dictionaries, for quick key search (first hash-bin, then linear search). For strings, an often used function is:

for c in some_string:

Take all of this with a grain of salt because it's been a while...

> Rendering with transparancy requires special rendering tricks, and with volume rendering even more.

If you want to do it correctly, yes it's a lot of work. But for a single trisurf like two brain hemispheres (our primary usecase over at MNE), naive transparency with backface culling and setting of blending modes is a good first-order solution (and would be necessary for a final version anyway). I have to imagine this is already exposed in `three.js` as it's pretty basic stuff.

IIRC VTK uses depth peeling, and I've never been able to enable it in `mayavi` in Python (how I interface with `vtk`) so I don't know how good it is.

The best solution AFAIK is the "weighted order independent transparency" two-pass rendering technique. There is discussion [here](https://github.com/vispy/vispy/issues/984) and [here](https://github.com/vispy/vispy/wiki/Tech.-Transparency) with a working demo [in this fairly readable gist](https://gist.github.com/liubenyuan/3739c343b3e6ca8117f7). I don't know for sure if it works with volume rendering, but I think it's supposed to be general enough so my guess is that it does. This would need to be done at the `three.js` level, but presumably that's true of all of these more advanced methods.

> Why you want to deprecate USBPollerThread?

Because its job is to poll non-libusb descriptors, only abstracting the libusb descriptor dance. It was not intended to be used where the simpler loop would do.

From there:
- I could extend USBPollerThread to type-check against epoll to detect problematic uses. Which would be very unpythonic.
- I could add a USBThread implementing the simpler loop. But it's so trivial to implement, and thread stop condition could vary across host applications that I prefer to let them implement it (code duplication should be minimal - if there is anything I would prefer to abstract better in python-libusb1 to reduce host application's boilerplate code it would be device enumeration).

OTOH, maybe I should extend USBPoller to:
- implement bits of the more complex loop to at least improve the situation when poller supports live descriptor set changes
- find a way to support kqueue too

@fake-name Yes and no - Selenium uses WebDriver (which is just a protocol specification) through ChromeDriver, which is the thing that ties the WebDriver protocol to the DevTools protocol. But ChromeDriver also does other things with windows, tabs and whatnot, and so insists on launching it's own windowful Chrome/Chromium. It doesn't (yet) work with --headless and I didn't find relevant tickets mentioning someone is actively working on it. Also, ChromeDriver/Chrome have this stupid limitation that if you do a screenshot you only capture the visible area, so I'm reduced to launching Chrome with a 2000px high window. You also run into other weird issues that you have to work around. So I believe using a --headless Chrome/Chromium using the plain DevTools API (or even implement (a subset/superset of) the WebDriver protocol that does saner things straight with the DevTools API is going to be so much better.

Hello,

Method [schema_mode()](https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/4609dc17e8ea04033984aa0f06c4d583ecf16aca/flask_restplus/namespace.py#L146) makes use of class [SchemaModel](https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/4609dc17e8ea04033984aa0f06c4d583ecf16aca/flask_restplus/model.py#L225), while [model()](https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/4609dc17e8ea04033984aa0f06c4d583ecf16aca/flask_restplus/namespace.py#L136) makes use of [Model](https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/4609dc17e8ea04033984aa0f06c4d583ecf16aca/flask_restplus/model.py#L118).

Class _Model_ inerhits _fields_ from _OrderedDict_ and fills it with the items of the dict passed to _model_().
Method [marshal()](https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/4609dc17e8ea04033984aa0f06c4d583ecf16aca/flask_restplus/marshalling.py#L13) walks over _items_ by assuming they're [fields objects](https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/master/flask_restplus/fields.py).

Class _SchemaModel_ does not deal with _fields_ at all.

That to say you can't marshal anything with schema models.

What do you mean Blueprint returns JSON when appropriate? I removed `app.response_class = JsonResponse` and it returned the text as `text/html`.

The purpose of `JsonResponse` is to set the `Content Type` to `application/json`. Without the `app.response_class = JsonResponse` the type of the response is:

```
Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
```

Example cURL without `app.response_class = JsonResponse`
```
$ curl --verbose --header "Accept: application/json" http://127.0.0.1:8000/hello
> Accept: application/json
< Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
```

Example cURL with `app.response_class = JsonResponse`:
```
$ curl --verbose --header "Accept: application/json" http://127.0.0.1:8000/hello
> Accept: application/json
< Content-Type: application/json

OR

curl --verbose http://127.0.0.1:8000/hello
> Accept: */*
< Content-Type: application/json
```

But, second: I have thought about it a bit, and I think I am against the idea of hiding the placeholders. The whole meaning of a logger `a.b.c` is that `c` is two levels deeper than `a`, not one level deeper. And:

1. The documentation in the Standard Library leads users to believe this.
2. The actual data structure really does have an `a.b` object — it is not a fiction, or something we should ignore, because there is really truly an object there.
3. The fact that `a` and `c` might be real logging nodes while `b` might be a `PlaceHolder` is a secret implementation detail, never mentioned in the Standard Library documentation, and unknown to users.
4. It is purely for the sake of efficiency that the logging module will use `c.parent` to “route around” `a.b` if `a.b` has never been accessed yet with a `getLogger` call. But we should still show `a.b` because, as far as real users know, it is a logger like any other, and in fact if they ask for it with `getLogger('a.b')` then it will instantly exist and be positioned in the hierarchy exactly where the user expected (and those secret `parent` attributes will be rewritten to include it).

So I think that `a.b.c` should still be shown two levels below `a` with `a.b` sitting in between them. Otherwise we will be breaking compatibility with both the Standard Library documentation, with the semantics of the `logger` module, and with the mental model that users have of their logging configuration.

However — the fact that a mainstream package like Celery is using the secret undocumented `parent` attribute to do something nefarious is certainly not something that we should hide from users!

I am imagining something like this:

```
   o<--[www]

      Broken .parent attribute! Points to: x.y.z
       |
       o<--"www.status"
           Level DEBUG
           Handler File '/foo/log.txt'
           Handler <MyHandler>
```

I have very similar need to access `aiohttp.Application` instance as it bears couple of properties to use in handling the event in Namespace class.

We have resolved that by adding:


	class MyNamespace(socketio.AsyncNamespace):
	    def __init__(self, namespace):
		super().__init__(namespace=namespace)
		self.app = None

	    async def on_connect(self, sid, environ):
		self.app = environ["aiohttp.request"].app
		# continue handling on_connect as needed

	    def on_disconnect(self, sid):
                # no need to remove app as it stays constant and only one
                pass

	    async def on_my_broadcast_event(self, sid, message):
	        # here using all the stuff attached to .app
		app = self.app
		devs_manager = app["devs_manager"]
		with await app["redis_pool"] as redis:
		    snap = await devs_manager.get_snapshot(redis)
		await self.emit('my response', {'data': snap})

I hope, we do it well.

The reason I note it here is to demonstrate, that accessint either `request` or `app` from code handling SocketIO events is rather common requirement.

```
from kvstore_server import KVStoreServer

def create(name='local', data_type='float32'):
    if not isinstance(name, string_types):
        raise TypeError('name must be a string')
    handle = KVStoreHandle()
    check_call(_LIB.MXKVStoreCreate(c_str(name), c_str(data_type),
                                    ctypes.byref(handle)))
    kv = KVStore(handle)
    is_worker = ctypes.c_int()
    check_call(_LIB.MXKVStoreIsWorkerNode(ctypes.byref(is_worker)))
    if is_worker.value == 0:
        server = KVStoreServer(kvstore)
        server.run()
        sys.exit()
    return kv
```

A way to check whether or not cython is actually used at runtime is seeing `mx.nd._internal.NDArrayBase`. It must be `mxnet._cy3.ndarray.NDArrayBase` if cython is used and `mxnet._ctypes.ndarray.NDArrayBase` otherwise.

So, if it is desirable, we could run a test like this before the unit tests run.
```bash
if [ "$(echo -e 'import mxnet as mx\nprint(mx.nd._internal.NDArrayBase)' | python)" != "<class 'mxnet._cy3.ndarray.NDArrayBase'>" ]; then
    echo "Cython is not used."
    exit 1
fi
```

One issue for us: this layer is an 'output layer' and hence doesn't support out grads into the layer. This is an unfortunate restriction, as it rules out things like example weighting. In addition, it does not return the actual per-example loss, which is non-trivial to compute. I think (but stand to be corrected, @piiswrong) that MXNet is moving away from these `output layers`. 

One option: split this into a `yolo_region_layer` (based on DarkNet https://github.com/pjreddie/darknet/blob/master/src/region_layer.c), and then a separate loss layer that returns the loss.

What are others thoughts on this? (could also have this 'output layer' and then also the separated region and loss layers, but this seems like a lot of code duplication).

Something important to keep in mind -- the current version of `execute_async()` just requires the task key (a bunch of strings) and the command that runs the task (something like `airflow run task`). That's because we make no assumptions about how the execution is implemented, or on what machine/environment, or even when. Passing a task object to the `execute_async()` method is contrary to that assumption. In particular, there is no guarantee that task objects are serializable.

The `key` should give enough information to load the task inside the executor by querying the database for the matching taskinstance -- I think that would be far preferable way to implement the desired behavior.

@mikepurvis reading the linked issue I see that you're trying to initialize the client using ``SwaggerClient.from_url('http://localhost:8888/api/spec.yaml')``.
While invoking ``from_url`` bravado will fetch the swagger spec file (yaml file in your case), read it and initialize the ``bravado-core`` ``Spec`` object.

According to the reported stack trace I see that ``pyyaml`` library is failing in decoding the yaml content.
Is possible that the file returned by your URL is not a valid yaml file?
If so I guess that you fixed your problem, if you think that the file is correct I would suggest to look into known issues of [``pyyaml``](https://github.com/yaml/pyyaml) or at least give us information about the file content (if possible).

The main difference of the init methods is:
 * ``from_spec`` expects the swagger specs in python dictionary format
 * ``from_url`` fetches the swagger specs from a url (like in your case), decode them into python dictionary and call ``from_spec``

Thanks for the bug report.

This is both a bug in the JSON store and bad design of the store interface. When we load a binary file, we basically try and load it with the JSON store first to see if it's already encrypted, and check if the JSON store returns any errors. At the point of loading, the binary store does not know whether it's loading an encrypted or unencrypted file. This is a design error. In these cases, the JSON store does not return any errors, because it sees `"" ""` (and others) as a valid JSON file (it shouldn't). Because it doesn't return any errors, the binary store just returns what the JSON store returns. The JSON store reads `"" ""` as "no data", so that's what's returned. For this reason, you encrypted file is actually empty, and then decrypting doesn't work.

So, to fix this, either:

1. Redesign the Store interface and make it aware of whether it's loading/emitting an encrypted or unencrypted file
2. Make the JSON store return an error when presented with malformed input such as these examples.

(Ideally both 😉 )

> Could the API potentially be exactly the same and the implementation switching based only on whether the the target and handlers functions are coroutines? Or would we need something more explicit?

Yes, API can left exactly the same. You can see in #23 that API of `on_predicate`/`on_exception` hasn't changed (they are still normal decorators with the same parameters as in `backoff`).

> Do you know of any other libraries that support something similar? i.e. is there a best practice out there already for this sort of thing?

Other libraries that implements exponential and other backoffs functionality? I haven't found any compatible with asyncio.

> If the decorated target is a coroutine, does that imply that the handlers have to be too? Can those be mix and matched?

Handlers can be regular functions even if target is coroutine.
From coroutines you can freely call regular functions with the exception that those functions must be non-blocking (i.e. `time.sleep()`, long computations, blocking networking are not allowed).
In #23 I explicitly convert handlers to coroutines, which should work assuming that handler doesn't do any blocking operations.

> Some keywords args can also be callable so their values can be evaluated at runtime. Would we have to or want to support async for those as well?

It depends. Support both sync and async versions of them is easy, but I don't think it's feasible to have them async --- is meaningful for them to read/compute value from network/database (i.e. value obtaining may require async operation)?

> I'm inclined to explore if we could roll async support into the core library (option 1. above) but I don't yet have a clear sense of what that entails. I'm quite fond of the current API and I wouldn't want to do it if it ended up polluting it with clunky options or an undue expansion of its surface.

External interface can be left the same, as seen in #23 . But implementation of `on_predicate`/`on_exception` will be either messy with conditions/switches, or be duplicated.
I see that `on_predicate` and `on_exception` shares a lot in common, but you haven't merged their implementations (probably because it will lead to messy code).

If we decide to left sync and async implementations of `on_predicate`/`on_exception` in separate function it should be quite easy to implement. Something like:

```python
def on_predicate(...):
    ...

    def decorate(target):
        @functools.wraps(target)
        def retry_sync(*args, **kwargs):
            ...

        @functools.wraps(target)
        @asyncio.coroutine
        def retry_async(*args, **kwargs):
            ...

        if asyncio.iscouroutinefunction(target):
            return retry_async
        else:
            return retry_sync
```

IMO I see two things relatively complicated:

1. Trying to extract common functionality from sync/async versions of `on_predicate`/`on_exception`. Or forget about it and accurately maintain four versions of `retry`.

2. Properly handle old Pythons, where asyncio is not available in `backoff.py`, tests, CI, etc.

There are currently no plans for the driver to support multiprocessing. This module is often used to improve the performance of CPU-bound work, whereas multithreading is often used instead for I/O-bound work, such as database access.

It's non-trivial allow `Driver` to work multiprocess as sockets cannot be pickled and the `Driver` hosts a connection pool.

Therefore multithreading may be a good choice in your case. If not, your only option is to move `Driver` construction inside the process:

```
def run(query, return_):
        graph = GraphDatabase.driver(...)
        with graph.session() as session:
            query_result = session.run(
                query
            )
        return_["answers"] = [rec for rec in query_result]
```

I think your `driver` object management is incorrect. You are defining the `driver` above `__init__` at class definition time. This creates _one driver instance_ that is shared over _all instances of your class_. This is very unlikely to be the scope you intended and is different from what may look like similar code in, say, Java.

Instead, the `driver` should be created _inside_ the `__init__` method, so that you get one `driver` per class instance.

I disagree that this shouldn't be fixed within Boto3/core, even if the REST API behaves this way.  Having one special region that should be assumed default and raises a confusing Exception when explicitly named violates the [Zen of Python](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/)

`Explicit is better than implicit`

Creating a boto3.client("ec2") will raise an exception for not specifying a region_name, so it's not as if the rest of Boto3 assumes us-east-1 as default.

Of course it's trivial for me just to add the below quirk now that I know of this behavior:

```
conn.create_bucket(
  BucketName='whatevs', 
  CreateBucketConfiguration=None if region == 'us-east-1' else {'LocationConstraint': region})
```

But why not add the quirk to Boto3's s3.create_bucket() instead of forcing every developer that ever creates S3 buckets to discover the behavior and re-implement the quirk in their own code?  

I haven't looked at the Boto3/core code yet, but I would imagine that adding something like this would fix it and prevent numerous other people from running into the same issue:

```
if CreateBucketConfiguration is not None and \
CreateBucketConfiguration["LocationConstraint"] == "us-east-1":
    CreateBucketConfiguration = None
```

I think the fix is actually pretty easy -- distutils will also read from a [`setup.cfg` in the current directory](https://docs.python.org/3/install/index.html#location-and-names-of-config-files), so you can create that and run the `pip install` from that directory!

This is a minimal diff that seems to work:

```diff
diff --git a/src/shiv/cli.py b/src/shiv/cli.py
index d95075d..dbae8e1 100644
--- a/src/shiv/cli.py
+++ b/src/shiv/cli.py
@@ -1,4 +1,5 @@
 import importlib_resources  # type: ignore
+import os
 import shutil
 import sys
 import uuid
@@ -125,10 +126,13 @@ def main(
     interpreter = validate_interpreter(python)
 
     with TemporaryDirectory() as working_path:
+        with Path(working_path, "setup.cfg").open('w') as f:
+            f.write('[install]\nprefix=')
         site_packages = Path(working_path, "site-packages")
         site_packages.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
 
         # install deps into staged site-packages
+        os.chdir(working_path)
         pip.install(
             python or sys.executable,
             ["--target", site_packages.as_posix()] + list(pip_args),
```

The ideal situation seems to me if we had a structure like the following:

middleware ingress -> tm manager -> pyramid ingress -> excview -> main

Basically for each attempt the request bubbles out of pyramid and back into middleware. The issue that on the final attempt we want to render a pretty error using exception views. Pylons had a crazy middleware that would call back into the app to render the error after the error propagated out. A way around that is if the TM injected into the environ a bit of info about whether this is the last attempt (`environ['tm.last_attempt'] = True`). If so, we can catch it in an excview and render a response instead of bubbling the exception out of the app. This avoids any issues with swapping out the request object after pyramid ingress (which just doesn't feel so great but is currently what we're discussing).

The excview logic is basically exactly what's in this PR, except with the predicate `is_tm_retryable` returning `False` if we are on the last attempt to avoid converting it to a `RetryableError` and instead allowing an exception view to handle it and render a response. The TM would then return the response but abort the transaction.

Interestingly this design does not require the new `invoke_exception_view` api. Unfortunately to accomplish this you need support from middleware which is out of scope of what is possible using `config.include`. We could consider `config.add_middleware(app_filter)` but I don't like doing that for several reasons. The old `repoze.tm2` was middleware, however this is worse because we need to add middleware **and** app-level config so you'd need to add a pipeline to your ini and setup `pyramid.includes`.

Where can we find a middleground? Is swapping the request out inside a tween the best we can do?

I have reworked this PR to be almost feature complete. It now makes a copy of the request on subsequent attempts, handles threadlocals pointing to the new request and some other quirks of making a new request object.

I've added a new predicate `tm_last_attempt=True/False` which user-code can use to register exceptions only for one case or the other if they wish. I plan to add another as well that would be `tm_exc_is_retryable=True/False` which will be capable of telling you if the exception is retryable if for some reason you want to know. This would allow you to add an exception view for something like `context=Exception, tm_exc_is_retryable=True` to render an appropriate error response for a failed retryable exception which would suggest to the user to try again.

Also the `request.environ` contains 3 keys: `tm.active`, `tm.attempt` and `tm.attempts` which can be used to determine where you're at in the cycle, as requested by @miohtama.

I would greatly appreciate some review of this as it will likely affect your applications.

This is because of how the launcher (```py.exe```) behaves. The documentation at https://docs.python.org/3.6/using/windows.html#shebang-lines says that ```#!/usr/bin/env python3``` is a valid shebang, and it runs Python 3, but will search ```PATH``` before using the default Python 3 version. The problem is that there's no "python3" command on ```PATH``` (Windows doesn't use the versioned names) so the ```PATH``` search will fail, and you'll get the default version of Python 3, which will be the system install, *not* your virtualenv.

I agree this is somewhat confusing, and it may even be that it's not what "should" happen (although it's difficult to think how else it could behave, given that there's no way short of running it to tell what version a given ```python.exe``` is). But it is behaving as documented.

If you want to query the behaviour of ```py.exe```, you should raise an issue on ```bugs.python.org``` (and link back to this issue for context) but there's no pip issue here, as far as I can see.

I believe you are correct.  I do not see how the local variable `derefAliases` is ever used.  

The `SearchByTreeWalkingMixin.search()` in `entryhelpers.py` creates `iterator` to walk the directory subtree specified by the search (base, onelevel, or subtree). It collects the matching nodes.

Dereferencing aliases seems like it would have to happen as you retrieved each result.  I'd say you'd need to wrap the local `matchCallback` function so that each result was dereferenced.  

RFC4511 [1] states that you need to check for dereference looping.
RFC4512 [2] does say that "An alias entry shall have no subordinates, so that an alias entry is always a leaf entry.", so that *might* help simplify tracking aliases.

[2] https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4512#section-2.6

You've misunderstood how `styles` option works. You can use it to choose from predefined representations styles only. The README contains complete list of them. For !!null type there are only "canonical", "lowercase", "uppercase", and "camelcase" styles.

Currently, null can not be represented as an empty node, and you can't just define another style for that because the dumper itself doesn't know how to represent empty nodes. Probably, this can be considered as a missed feature. However, I don't like the idea of representing null as an empty node. It's kind of type information loss.

If we create a new Type option to explicitly specify NOT to apply styling during dump, then the assumption is that by default the folding styling will be applied to other custom Types. 

I don't think that's the right assumption - we should expect that the proper string serialization output would be produced by the custom Type "represent" function. 

I agree more with Vitaly on this point that the style/folding is only safe with "string" and that's the only type that such folding/style should be applied. 

If we DO add a new meta option to Type, then it should be to state that the new custom Type is OK to apply folding/style, not the other way.

I don't think you can add `:conf` to the bolt in order to specify tick tuples. I believe they are a topology-wide setting (thus the `topology.` prefix). However, you can flip tick tuples on:

    sparse run -o "topology.tick.tuple.freq.secs=1" -t 10 -d

In this case, you end up seeing the following in the debug output:

    11960 [Thread-22-count-bolt] INFO  backtype.storm.daemon.executor - Execute done TUPLE source: __system:-1, stream: __tick, id: {}, [2] TASK: 4 DELTA:

We discussed how to handle this. We'll have a method called `process_tick()` that only gets called when the tuple is actually tick tuple, and ticks won't get passed to `process()`, because otherwise they mess with unpacking logic and such. @cabiad says he's going to try this one for size at PyCon.

I am +1 to `Component.spec(**parameters)` over `Component(**parameters)` for topology definitions. Add @dan-blanchard's improvements:

* [ ] Match parameter names to those in the thrift spec.
* [ ] Support referencing a spec by object name when handling inputs.
* [ ] Support class attributes for default parameters which are typically decided when writing the component class (see below).

Reviewing this thread from [this comment](#issuecomment-93054609) on, topics are:

* Object-Oriented Design
* Domain-Specific Language Design
* Topology Parameters


*Object-Oriented Design*

Using `Component(**parameters)` breaks at least two [SOLID](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SOLID_(object-oriented_design)) principles, most notably the *single responsibility principle*. It would have the `Component` both represent the task within the topology and its place within the topology, as @amontalenti points out. While users never instantiate these classes directly, it is useful to understand Storm's [entities](http://streamparse.readthedocs.org/en/master/topologies.html#parallelism-and-workers) and how the `Component` class leads to a task in Storm.

About subclassing `Component` without calling `__init__`: the guidance to users is "Don't do that." There should be no problem in using `super`, and it's a well-known pattern.


*Domain-Specific Language Design*

The differences in the DSL itself between the two approaches:

* Remove five characters for each component spec. (-1 from me.)
* Match parameter names to those in the thrift spec. (+1 from me.)
* Support referencing a spec by object name when handling inputs. (+1 from me.)


*Topology Parameters*

There are some parameters in the topology spec which are attributes of the component, not of the topology, at least in terms of sensible defaults. When writing a component, you are going to decide on (default) naming conventions for attributes like `output_fields` and `group_on`.

* Support class attributes for default parameters which are typically decided when writing the component class. (+1 from me.)

Attributes like `parallelism_hint` are truly topology attributes, but since it's called a "hint," I could see how users would want to include that on the component. That said, the topology spec conveys more complete information if it explicitly includes input/output and parallelism.

There are several issues with this approach. 
From the beginning I wasn't sure that cutting up a polygon to triangles was the right way to do it. Rectangular apertures are special cases and are used on their own, but noone requested to have triangular ones. It would be good not to go down the path of having all the special cases of triangles/rectangles/pentagons/hexagons/..../, but only have one and only complex, arbitrary polygon class. So while we need a polygon class I can be OK with the idea of basing it on the triangular one until a better implementation is written.

But: I wouldn't merge anything half baked, a user facing API, and the aperture class needs to be put into this PR. We don't have functions that neither have a public API nor used in a public facing function/class etc, and I would stick with that rule. Keeping the codebase of any project is quite a task on it's own, and no need to build in cruft that may or may not become functional code at some point.

Also, any new feature needs to have narrative and docstring documentation as well as tests to be attached.

The other thing is that there were quite a lot of expansion and improvement on the new ``regions`` affiliated package during the last week. Basically it was decided that the core of the aperture photometry (the geometry and masks) should be moved over there and photutils will only keep the photometry related methods (basically the flux summing) and will use ``regions`` it as a dependency. 

Thus I'm cc-ing @astrofrog and @keflavich. Do you think this should go straight to ``regions``?

I had the chance to look at the possibilities to read exif metadata with python. Here are the options that I show in my lecture:

Wth pillow

```from PIL import Image
from PIL.ExifTags import TAGS


img = Image.open(img_path)
info = img._getexif() # returns a dictionnary
for k, v in info.items():
    # TAGS is a dictionnary containing human-readable entries
    nice = TAGS.get(k, k)
    print('%s (%s) = %s' % (nice, k, v))
```

With èxifread

```
import exifread


img = open(img_path, 'rb')

# Return Exif tags
tags = exifread.process_file(img)
print(tags)
```

with pyexiftool

Not available on pip :(

This is basically a wrapper on the excellent [exiftool](https://sno.phy.queensu.ca/~phil/exiftool/) (Windows, Linux, MacOS). A good idea to reuse this code instead of writing yours. It's a unique file that you can copy paste in the source tree of your project for the sake of easiness.

[Documentation](http://smarnach.github.io/pyexiftool/)

```
import exiftool

files = [img_path, img_path]
with exiftool.ExifTool() as et:
    # Single file...
    metadata = et.get_metadata(img_path)
    # Or a batch of files
    metadata = et.get_metadata_batch(files)
    
print(metadata)

```

And no, I don't see a way around this change. One is obviously a bug because `'\n'.join([b'list', b'of', b'bytestrings'])` is definitely no good.
And the `line = None` part prevents another obscure bug that you may see in the logs:
> the use of non-builtin globals isn't supported
There is some weird function serialization going on, and it thinks that `line` is a global because it's used in the function without previously being assigned.

I don't want to intrude but I was reading over some comments and I just wanted to respectfully interject that in general it is a really bad practice for code to have "personality". What I mean is, configurations that mean one thing in one place and something else in another - or the same state or condition being "interpreted" differently across different implementations within the same body or module of code/code files... 

The user in the above case then needs to "acquire" and memorize conditions for each code/module sharing some common (but differently interpreted) state. Requiring devs to have "insider knowledge" violates conceptual encapsulation and also is kind of a pain in the butt... :-)  "Secret incantations" generally suck to have to deal with... Just an opinion guys...

> [15, 5, 3] -->1553 --> SCalarEncoder(1553) --> 010...01

I don't think this is the right approach, because the context of the individual frequency band counts is lots when `[15, 5, 3]` is squeezed into `1553`. For instance, if `3` changed to `30`, the number goes from 1553 to 15530, which represents a huge distance from 1553, when actually two of the values didn't change. The nature of SDRs means that your data will be misrepresented here.

Can you explain why you need it?

There's no notion of "matching cells" in the Temporal Memory algorithm. When learning in a bursting column, if any existing segments are a partial match, we'll learn on that segment rather than growing a new one. These partial matches are termed "matching segments".

If you want a list of cells that have matching segments, you can call tm.connections.mapSegmentsToCells() on the return value of tm.getMatchingSegments(). Though this list might contain duplicates.

>> cliquet.permission_url
> 
> If I use memory I guess this is not required?

For memory, it can be ignored indeed (@Natim got confused with `cliquet.permission_backend`)
But he is right about not using it in production :)

>> However (and the documentation might be really bad about this), you have to use a ProtectedResource and a RouteFactory
>
> Well I was missing this information.

I am sorry, my turn to be wrong. This is not mandatory actually, I just realized that we set it up automatically [when the protected resource is registered](https://github.com/mozilla-services/cliquet/blob/master/cliquet/resource/viewset.py#L206-L209).

>> Permissions are only provided on single record endpoints with the current code.
> 
> Things are getting confused. We have permissions on record and on collections, right?

Yes. But the `permissions` attribute is only present in the payloads of the record endpoints (doc is wrong, I will work on it). That means that permissions management via JSON API is only available at the record level.
The permissions management at the collection level is done using settings in configuration.

> With champion_read_principals = system.Everyone everyone can see every records when they do 
> GET /champions. Who can read records of this collection?

If everyone can read the collection, everyone can read every records of the collection. Every one can `GET /champions/id`. This is a kind of inheritance yes.

What we also provide by default, is the ability to only see the records where you have read access.
For example, in the configuration, you only set `champion_write_principals = system.Authenticated`. Thus everyone can create records. And when they `GET /champions` they only see the records where they have read permissions (the ones they created, or that someone shared with them, or shared with system.Authenticated/Everyone...)

> When I create a new record, what are the default permission on this record?

The default permission is `{"write": ["<current user-id>"]}`. (*and write includes read*)

> Is there a way to change this default?

By inheriting the classes in `cliquet/resource` and plugging them yes. We would be very happy if you contribute something to adjust default permissions without overriding classes :)

> For example I want all my users to see all champions (/champions) but they can only get their own
> records with /champions/id. 

Ok, that's because of your secret fields, right ? You want everyone to be able to list every records and their *public* fields. But when they get a single record they can see the *private* fields if the record belongs to them.

That is feasible indeed but you have to inherit and customize the behaviour when returning payloads. It is not covered by our code currently. Or using two resources maybe...  `GET /champions-secret?rel_record=abcd-5678-3090`

> With Cliquet there are no inheritance by default, you must provide a inheritance function.
> See for instance what we did for Kinto here: https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/master/kinto/authorization.py
> 
I don't think this is your use-case. That's when you have several resources and want to give permissions to one from the permissions you have on the others. In Kinto we use it to have like cascading permissions...
