There's discussion on colander to remove arbitrary keywords on the `__init__` and that change would effectively break the `location` and `type` keywords being used on the colander schemas.  I've yet to really understand the point in the `type` keyword over just using proper colander types so I don't think removing that is really that big a deal (until I figure out why it was added in the first place).  The `location` functionality could be replaced by making all validation schemas look something like:

```
class ValidationSchema(MappingSchema):
    querystring = SchemaNode()
    headers = SchemaNode()
    body = SchemaNode()
    path = SchemaNode()
```

So, each of the 4 "locations" would be represented by children of the validation schema.

That's the change I'd like to see done.

I guess one could argue that it might be a good thing to skip the extra work (presumably) done by newTransaction if a new transaction is implicitly begun after a commit (say in a multi-step web request, if you're sure things are in sync/don't care if they are). I kind of think I was hearing that a little bit over on ZODB#56, I think.

But I would argue that in that case, that's far out of the normal expected transaction semantics and so you want a custom transaction manager (or use transaction objects directly).

I solved this problem. In your service you just need to put your service in the background; but remember now it can be killed to recliam memory.
Here is the code that goes in your service/main.py:
```python
from jnius import autoclass
Service = autoclass('org.renpy.android.PythonService').mService
Service.stopForeground(True)
```
Here is the Java Documentation for that call: http://developer.android.com/reference/android/app/Service.html#stopForeground%28boolean%29

First, you could pass the dict directly to Fire, rather than passing a class:
```python
if __name__ == '__main__':
    fire.Fire(App.show())
```

This way you can run `python app.py` instead of `python app.py show`

```bash
$ python app.py
safe: safe!
```

-----

Second, a simple alternative that you might like is renaming `dangerous` to `_dangerous`. This will make it hidden by default, only visible if the --verbose flag is used. Note that it can still be called from the command line. If it's not _too_ dangerous, this is my recommended solution.

```
import fire

class Obj(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.safe = 'safe!'

    def _dangerous(self):
        print('dangerous!')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    fire.Fire(Obj)
```

-----

Another alternative is to use a class (instead of a dict) with just the safe stuff:
```
class App(object):

   def safe(self):
      obj = Obj()
      return obj.safe

if __name__ == '__main__':
    fire.Fire(App)
```

I don't recommend this though because it requires keeping App up to date with Obj.

```Python
def downside_dev(returns, required_ROR=0):
    mask = returns > required_ROR
    dsides = returns.mask(mask, 0)
    sq_devs = np.square(dsides)
```

FYI, we added [support for this](https://github.com/kasei/attean/blob/master/bin/canonicalize_bgp.pl) in the [Attean](https://github.com/kasei/attean) perl library to help with caching. It was a fairly simply extension of existing graph canonicalization code to handle any set of patterns (triples, triple patterns, quads, quad patterns, SPARQL Results...).

```
% cat test3.rq
PREFIX foaf: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/>
SELECT * WHERE {
    ?p a foaf:Person ; foaf:name ?name
}

% perl bin/canonicalize_bgp.pl test3.rq
# Hash key: f3e47550cdcf8c0bd1c93f55f01f9b1a73db9efd
SELECT (?v001 AS ?p) (?v002 AS ?name) WHERE {
	?v001 <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type> <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/Person> .
	?v001 <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/name> ?v002 .
}
```

It looks like this problem is happening when points adjacent to the initially frozen points are updated for the first time, before the main marching algorithm starts. In this simple example,


```python
import numpy as np
import skfmm
phi = np.array([[1, -1, -1],
                [0,  0, -1],
                [0,  0,  1]])

speed = np.array([[ 1, value, 1],
                  [ 0, 0,     1],
                  [ 0, 0,     1]])

phi = np.ma.MaskedArray(phi,phi==0)
tt =  skfmm.travel_time(phi,speed,order=order)
```

as `value` decreases from 1 the arrival time at point (0,2) increase up to a local maximum and then goes asymptotically to zero. For second-order this local maximum occurs at `value=1/2.` and for first-order `value=1/3`. For second-order, below `value=1/2.` the up-wind finite difference approximation along the x direction, used to estimate the travel-time gradient, falls below 1/2 which may be physically incorrect. For first order, below `value=1/3.` the finite-difference approximation in the x direction falls below zero.

> In the case of initdb, on Debian-style packaging, it makes sense to use pg_createcluster instead.

Absolutely not agree, `pg_createcluster` is a simple perl script, which calls the old good `initdb` at the end. Personally I've never seen postgres packages, were all postgres binaries are not located in the same directory. All Ubuntu/Debian/RedHat/CentOS/SUSE/Gentoo/YourFavoriteDistro "magic-wrapper-scripts", i.e. pg_createcluster/pg_upgradecluster/pg_ctlcluster/etc are not doing anything except calling the original postgres binary (initdb/pg_upgrade/pg_ctl/etc). Taking into account that such wrappers are not available for other systems, I think it's not worth to spend time on using them.

@hjacobs My use case doesn't involve very much processing but the model's schema is somewhat complex. It doesn't make much sense to break the API into smaller microservices (IMO).

I've started working on a PR where the swagger document's external references are resolved into a single dictionary before being passed to the swagger validator. For example:
```yaml
# doc1.yaml
key1: value1
key2:
  $ref: 'doc2.yaml#/key3'

# doc2.yaml
key3: value3
```

will resolve to a dictionary that looks like
```python
{'key1': 'value1', 'key2': 'value3'}
```

Recursive references will be handled by pointing the recursive `$ref` to its new location in the combined document. Do you guys see any issues with this approach? Also, I don't plan on implementing non-local reference resolution (e.g. using http).

The main thing is the difference between [how re.match() and re.search() works](https://docs.python.org/2/library/re.html#search-vs-match) ([same question on stackoverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/180986/what-is-the-difference-between-pythons-re-search-and-re-match)).
Ex.:
```re.match('1.*', '213')``` # dont match

```re.search('1.*', '213')``` # match
```<_sre.SRE_Match object at 0x7f92a5535510>```

If you want it to work as before - you have to specify '^' in the rule. And it wouldn't take effect in re.match() case.
Ex.:
```re.match('^1.*', '213')``` # dont match

```re.search('^1.*', '213')``` # dont match

So, no - it's not fully backwards-compatible.

Thanks for the suggestions @gamaanderson.

Just to make things clear, the Nyquist velocity cannot "change", once a spectrum is collected and moment calculated the Nyquist velocity is a set property for the particular gates (and typically ray and sweep).  What can change is what are good limited when plotting the Doppler velocities.  Raw velocities are limited by +/- the Nyquist velocity so these values are good limits when plotting.  If the velocities have been unfolded (dealiased) then these limits are no longer appropriate for plotting as the velocities will be outside of these limits if any folded gates were present in the volume.  Also, it should be noted that it is not uncommon for the Nyquist velocities reported in files to be incorrect, but there is little Py-ART can (and perhaps should) do in these cases.

That said, a method for determining reasonable default limits for unfolded Nyquist velocities would be a good addition to Py-ART.  I would be in favor of updating the function used for determining these limits for the `corrected_velocity` field most likely not the `velocity` field.  How these limits should be determined needs some discussion. Perhaps finding the integer multiple of the Nyquist velocity for which all velocities in the field are inside these limits would be a reasonable default.  Another option would be for the dealiasing routines in Py-ART to set these limits as `valid_min` and `valid_max` attributes in the field dictionary and then add logic to the `get_field_limits` to look for and use these values.  The `parse_vmin_vmax` function in pyart/graph/common already this logic for use in the **RadarDisplay** and related classes.

Also, the current spec is silent on whether it’s legal to give multiple `LigatureCaretByPos` or `LigatureCaretByIndex` statements for the same glyph. For example:
```
table GDEF {
    LigatureCaretByPos f_f_f_i 600 200;
    LigatureCaretByPos f_f_f_i 400;
} GDEF;
```

Both `makeotf` and `fontTools` allow this. These implementations collect the caret positions for each glyph and emit them in sorted order:
```
    <LigCaretList>
      <Coverage>
        <Glyph value="f_f_f_i"/>
      </Coverage>
      <!-- LigGlyphCount=1 -->
      <LigGlyph index="0">
        <!-- CaretCount=3 -->
        <CaretValue index="0" Format="1">
          <Coordinate value="200"/>
        </CaretValue>
        <CaretValue index="1" Format="1">
          <Coordinate value="400"/>
        </CaretValue>
        <CaretValue index="2" Format="1">
          <Coordinate value="600"/>
        </CaretValue>
      </LigGlyph>
    </LigCaretList>
```

How about saying the following in the spec?

> The same glyph can be specified in multiple `LigatureCaretByPos`, `LigatureCaretByIndex` and `LigatureCaretByDev` statements. For every glyph, implementations must collect all caret values and emit a merged `LigGlyph` table in `GDEF.LigCaretList`. Implementations may emit a warning if the number of caret values is different from the number of component glyphs (eg., inferred from the glyph name).

Since the order can change at runtime anyway (because hinting affects caret values in format 2), there probably is not much point in mandating any particular sorting order.

Thoughts?

Unless you're subsetting, as long as you are using the _-cff_ command-line option, meaning that the data remains CFF, glyph order should be unchanged. And, even if you are subsetting, there is an option, _-pg_, for preserving GIDs and their order, which presumably inserts dummy glyphs for the GIDs that are not included in the subset.

(This is one reason why I like CID-keyed fonts: the glyph order is inherent and cannot be changed without changing the underlying glyph-to-CID assignments, which requires using the _mergeFonts_ or _rotateFont_ tool and an appropriate mapping table.)

```python
from gcloud import storage
```

The intent for the "synchronous" query is to make it easy to handle "simple" queries which run in bounded time.  In that case, `query.complete` will be `True` after `query.run()`, and the first batch (or only batch, if the result is small enough) will be available as `query.rows`.

If the synchronous query times out, then the procedure you discovered (poll the `query.job` until it is finished) is correct.  At that point, you have to use `query.fetch_data` to get even the initial set of rows (because the query doesn't get access to the async results).

> When I tried to use query.job I found each time it returns a different job instance, which is confusing.

Given the expected usecase (use the job to poll until a timed-out query is complete), I didn't anticipate needing to ensure that multiple accesses to `query.job` would return the same instance. #1673 tracks this issue.

### From the perspective of the API

The UI "does" folders in the sense that it splits filenames on `/` characters (and has the concept of "creating a folder"), but it's really "folder fiction" in the sense that the API itself doesn't actually have any concept of a "folder".

The definition of `is_dir` would be:

```python
def is_dir():
  for f in get_files(prefix='/myfoldername/'):
    return True
  return False
```

That is... if there is an object called "/home/jj/file.txt" then "/home/jj" is a "folder". But you could create an object named "/home/jj/file.txt" wihtout creating the "folders" called "/home" or "/home/jj" -- which doesn't quite line up with the way Unix thinks of "directories" (/"folders")

### With a bit of magic

> This rule-based approach stands in contrast to the way many tools work, which create objects to mark the existence of folders (such as “dir_$folder$”)

- https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/gsutil/addlhelp/HowSubdirectoriesWork

You will need to create a pyinstaller hook for packages that depend on setuptools' get_distribution() to obtain metadata information about the package. 

Create a hook file in some hooks directory. The file name should be ``hook-<modulename>.py`` for pyinstaller to find it properly (i.e. ``hook-gcloud.py``).

The content will be:
```
from PyInstaller.utils.hooks import copy_metadata
datas = copy_metadata('google-cloud-core')
```

The hooks directory should be set in the Analysis phase of your pyinstaller .spec file:

```
a = Analysis(['bogus.py'],
             pathex=[],
             binaries=[],
             hiddenimports=[],
             hookspath="/path/to/your/hooks/dir",
             runtime_hooks=[],
             excludes=[],
             win_no_prefer_redirects=False,
             win_private_assemblies=False,
             cipher=block_cipher)
```

I haven't tested the above, but this is what worked for me for another python package which is doing something similar (APScheduler).

If and when you get this working, consider contributing the hook back to pyinstaller project.

The guest environment is only intended to run on GCE VMs with access to the metadata server. Startup and shutdown scripts intentionally wait for access to the metadata server to ensure proper configuration and cleanup take place. If you do not have access to the metadata server and want to shutdown your instance, you can either disable shutdown scripts or stop the VM from the API layer (which will automatically shut the VM down after 2 minutes).

Interesting idea, but `pickle` (and thus `dill`) pickle recursively, as @matsjoyce said.  Basically, if you try to pickle a `dict` with a function in it, then the function is pickled before the dict… and before the function is pickled, the underlying code object is pickled along with helper functions -- and the `globals` dictionary and any closures… and other stuff.  Then before the `globals` are pickled, if it's not one of the special cases `dill` handles, then it's another similar dive into that `dict`… before anything is pickled.  You can think of the pickle as a DAG (an acyclic graph), where the pickle doesn't happen until all the dependent objects are pickled inside it.

The only way I can see this recursive behavior being converted to streaming is that as `pickle` or `dill` go "down the DAG", you set aside that object to be stored individually… and when you go "up the DAG" (i.e. one of the dependencies is pickled) the dependency is pickled and "streamed" to wherever it's going to be streamed to.  I believe `pickle` is smart enough (i.e. `memo` does this) that if you've already "memorized" some object in the pickling process, `pickle` does not pickle it again.

So, it would be possible… however, it would take some work, as it's a fundamental change to the pickling mechanism (i.e. you are changing how `pickle` navigates the DAG).

A short-term workaround is to uninstall the android backend manually.  This would require adding a local pants plugin like so:

1. edits to pants.ini
 ```ini
[DEFAULT]
pythonpath: ["%(buildroot)s/pants"]

[backends]
packages: ["custom"]
 ```

2. new dir/file `mkdir -p pants/custom && touch pants/custom/__init__.py`

3. new file pants/custom/register.py
 ```python
import inspect
import logging

from pants.goal.goal import Goal


logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def register_goals():
  uninstall_android_tasks()


def uninstall_android_tasks():
  for goal in Goal.all():
    for name in goal.ordered_task_names():
      task_type = goal.task_type_by_name(name)
      for clazz in inspect.getmro(task_type):
        if clazz.__module__.startswith('pants.backend.android'):
          logger.debug('Uninstalling task {} {}'.format(name, task_type))
          goal.uninstall_task(name)
          break
 ```

I've been working away at the engine experiment this week and the base support for experiments are example graphs in json form.  This led to re-building a build graph parser and it seems like its both simpler and has more capabilities (non-target addressables just work).  It should be faster, but no proof of that.  It is fully lazy though.  You may or may not be interested but @benjyw did talk about working on parse speed.

The key tricks/differences from the current parsing scheme number 2:
1. All top-level objects in a build file are Serializable (namedtuple or namedtuple-like) - this allows the whole of the build graph to not care about more than this type, and...
2. Field values are now wrapped in the `addressable` and `addressables` functions to indicate values that can be pointers (addresses).   This is used in the Node (the new Target) constructor.

These 2 things allow for any ~plain old class to be a Node or a leaf (Addressable) in the graph and the parsing to be controlled.  The control comes in because `addressable` and `addressables` both support either objects of the expected types or addresses that are expected to point at objects of those type.  As such, re-constructing a "thin" object with `addressable` or `addressables` fields with hydrated values for those fields always just works, leading to the pattern:
1. parse json dict -> thin Serializable object instance with address references (or a mix of embedded objects)
2. when an object parsed in 1 needs to be resolved, re-construct the object by asking for its dict, iterating the values there, hydrating those that are addresses, then re-constructing the same object using the hydrated dict.

Hi @SamuelLarkin , thanks for trying out Sockeye!

Residual connections are always added to the transformer model, but there are some other options that can be relevant for successfully training transformers. You can take a look at the training script we published for the arxiv paper in the arxiv branch: https://github.com/awslabs/sockeye/blob/arxiv_1217/arxiv/code/transformer/sockeye/train-transformer.sh

These are some parameters that worked well on the WMT17 dataset in the paper:
```
--batch-type=word \
--batch-size=4096 \
--embed-dropout=0:0 \
--encoder=transformer \
--decoder=transformer \
--num-layers=6:6 \
--transformer-model-size=512 \
--transformer-attention-heads=8 \
--transformer-feed-forward-num-hidden=2048 \
--transformer-preprocess=n \
--transformer-postprocess=dr \
--transformer-dropout-attention=0.1 \
--transformer-dropout-relu=0.1 \
--transformer-dropout-prepost=0.1 \
--transformer-positional-embedding-type fixed \
--label-smoothing 0.1 \
--weight-tying \
--weight-tying-type=src_trg_softmax \
--num-embed 512:512 \
--clip-gradient=-1 \
--initial-learning-rate=0.0001 \
--learning-rate-reduce-num-not-improved=8 \
--learning-rate-reduce-factor=0.7 \
--weight-init xavier --weight-init-scale 3.0 \
--weight-init-xavier-factor-type avg
```

1) The service file is custom ... i.e., it is a subclass of `MXNetBaseService`
2) The load_checkpoint call is as follows:
```
        sym, arg_params, aux_params = mx.model.load_checkpoint(self.model_name, self.epoch_num)
        self.mod = mx.mod.Module(symbol=sym, context=mx.gpu(0))
        self.mod.bind(for_training=False, data_shapes=[('data', (1, 20))], label_shapes=[('softmax_label', (1, ))])
        self.mod.set_params(arg_params, aux_params, allow_missing=True, allow_extra=True)
```

Note that it's really all the artifacts that need to be in the mxnet-model-server directory ... not just the service file.

From the documentation, it seems the new `Cors` option in a SAM template can only be added on the `Properties` of a `Serverless::Api`-type resource.

Supposing you have a resource of `Type` = `AWS::Serverless::Function`, it would be great if you could write:
```
Resources:
  MyResource:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      ..
      Cors: "'*'"
      ..
```

From the docs

```
A resource of this type [AWS::Serverless::Api] is implicitly created from the union of Api events defined on AWS::Serverless::Function resources defined in the template that do not refer to an AWS::Serverless::Api resource.
```

suggesting it should be possible to pull in a `Cors` property from a AWS::Serverless::Function resource when auto-creating the Serverless:Api resource, and so to pass this through to the generated swagger file.

For simple scenarios like ours this would then let us simplify our config a lot — removing the swagger file and the Serverless::Api entry completely!

I'm making this simplified decision:

**Daemonization happens once everything is initialized and right before starting cherrypy**

## Initialization

Will try to gather everything in one place.. currently I see:

 * `django.setup()`
 * `collectstatic`
 * `collectstatic_js_reverse`
 * `migrate`

All these things may produce errors and are likely to have valuable console feedback for the user. Therefore at the current stage of daemonizing things, I don't think it's wise to fork and return before we're done with all these steps.

This also puts less weight on the logic of the startup lock. Dropping to daemon mode while doing potentially exclusive tasks and not displaying feedback really requires the startup lock.

I personally don't like the idea of storing CDXJ as such into IPFS. It can be done for the sake of storage, but not for the sake of discovery as the primary goal. However, I very much like to make the system free from the CDXJ and fully working on the IPFS only. The main functionality of the CDXJ is to allow lookup/discovery, which can be done differently such as how [Fluidinfo](https://github.com/fluidinfo/fluiddb) did it. This would require IPFS to support key-value store or linked data style node graph exploration capability.

One possible workflow would be to define a well known starting point for everything. In case of Fluidinfo it is `/about/{anything}`. So, `/about/example.com` would provide with a node that will have many tags (relations) pointing to different property nodes related to `example.com`. One of those property nodes could be pointing to the `TimeMap` node that further has connected `Memento` nodes and returns a double linked list or blockchain of mementos. The graph is illustrated in the following figure:

![img_20161209_114525](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/65147/21057042/ff264e62-be05-11e6-9140-6950fc391b1c.jpg)

An alternate approach would be to offload the `TimeMap` generation to the IPNS (which I think is planned/implemented to support Memento protocol to represent past versions). In this approach when a memento is resolved without any datetime, the IPNS will point to the latest `Memento`, but it can point to any previous version if the datetime is supplied. Additionally, the IPNS should be able to return all versions in the form of a `TimeMap`. The graph is illustrated in the following figure:

![img_20161209_114551](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/65147/21057204/a658686e-be06-11e6-9da9-bc2b6ba94682.jpg)

Each `Memento` node is further decomposed into request record, response headers, and response payload (if any). Additional attributes can be attached to annotate each Memento even further such as to specify which collection it belongs to, who archived it, the name of the WARC file (if the node was imported from a WARC file) and whatnot. To minimize the number of nodes, the main `Memento` node itself may store the request record, if request records are going to be unique each time. However, it is important to separate response payload from the response header for greater deduplication.

![img_20161209_114622](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/65147/21057231/c75ba26a-be06-11e6-8adf-b490b4ee5b2f.jpg)

This will even allow import and export of WARC files. Exporting to WARC file can be selective if the user wants to apply some filters. It is important to note that if such an archiving system is created, it is not necessary to use WARC based archiving, independent tools like browser extensions can push observation records (Mementos) into the system with added attributes such as session information and make it globally accessible. This way the archiving system will be fully decentralized and collaborative, both capture and replay.

/cc @jbenet for thoughts.

I think, the solution is far simpler than you might think. Basically, you can just hard code `app.run(host="0.0.0.0")`. You may use port here from the supplied arguments or environment variable, but keep the host argument as it is.

Actually, it's expected that the Exception should include the errorMessage as well. I can confirm that is not the case though; it seems this is a bug that was introduced with the enhanced logging.

Essentially, what is happening is that the same error's content is being read by both `_ExtractRequestSummaryFields` and `_ExtractError`. Since `_ExtractRequestSummaryFields` is done first, it has the correct content. On the other hand, `_ExtractError` is second, and consequently returns an empty string when reading the error's content.

Getting back to your question, yes, we can add this to the AdWordsReportError by resolving this bug. A temporary work-around may be to disable your logs if you really want this information in the Exception, but then you would have no logs... 

Regards,
Mark

The login token is kept in memory by the `Client` object, but it won't be available to the outside (including programs like `docker-compose`).

If you need to persist your credentials, you can either
- Use the `docker login` command.
- Write into the `config.json` file manually using python or some other script. `docker-py`, unfortunately, isn't designed for this task.

I don't think I like this idea. You can always do this:

```python

params = kwargs_from_env(...)
params.update(custom_params)
```

or, if you want to override defaults:

```python

params = {...}
params.update(kwargs_from_env(...))
```

Passing in defaults here is unnecessary, it makes it very difficult to expand the params of this function in the future.

Further investigation confirm that the spec defined by docker itself differs from what the Docker Python API does.

This is why: We're trying to make mock_helper.py work for all unit test cases. In reality, we should think of mock_helper.py as a helper that sets side effects for us in a way that works in 90% of the cases. Otherwise we risk making things too complicated.

The solution for unit tests in which mock_helper.py doesn't work as-is, is not to make mock_helper.py more complicated but to simply not use it and set side effects in the unit test itself.

@mscuthbert wrote:
> This assumes that v1 is (supposed to be) higher than v2 -- perhaps we should make that a requirement?

This remains an open question, and could lead to a broader discussion of the intended use and scope of this module.

The docstring for the class VoiceLeadingQuartet indicates that v1 should be the top voice, but some of the code in the module seems ambivalent about which voice is higher, and some of the docstring tests explicitly have examples in which V2 is higher. These examples resolve fine as much of the code works even if the input is upside down.

isProperResolution() and having any coherent notion of voice crossing depend on a required ordering of the voices. We might also think about the case of analysis of two equal voices that cross a lot, in which case the notion of a higher voice fades away.

Enforcing the correct order of voices might break backwards compatibility, as users might well have been putting the voices in backwards (as in the docstrings!) but getting correct results, since it doesn't matter which order you put the voices for parallelFifth, parallelOctave, etc.

diff --git a/src/pulp/pulp.py b/src/pulp/pulp.py
index 7bc6f12..c1852a7 100644
--- a/src/pulp/pulp.py
+++ b/src/pulp/pulp.py
@@ -1492,7 +1492,7 @@ class LpProblem(object):
         Side Effects:
             - The file is created.
         """
-        f = open(filename, "w")
+        f = open(filename, "w", encoding='utf-8')

             f.write("Minimize\n")

I have chosen 50mb as default value, because the docker-registry is using the LRU cache only to store registry meta data and no layers (~50kb per file) to eliminate round-trips to the storage layer and I think this should be enough. (The public docker hub was using a 2GB LRU cache to serve the world, when I implemented this)

> Once this feature is enabled, all small files (tags, meta-data) will be cached in Redis. When using a remote storage backend (like Amazon S3), it will speed things up dramatically since it will reduce roundtrips to S3.

Having a too small cache should only decrease the pulling speed and not cause any errors. Maybe the registry-to-registry transfer is somehow interfering with the cache (creates some keys it shouldn't) in a bad way.

```
ENDPOINTS=("/deis/domains" "/deis/builder/users")

        for key in $(etcdctl ls --recursive ${endpoint}); do
                value=$(etcdctl get ${key} 2>&1)
                if [[ $value != *directory ]]; then
                        echo "etcdctl set ${key} '${value}'"
                fi
        done
done
```

The need comes from the fact that trying to build an "identity set aware" application without this is impossible (and that's what Jim and I are trying to do, which is how #64 originally surfaced).

The only sensible alternative is to support more arbitrary parameter passthroughs, which is what Bryce liked for the `TransferClient` for a lot of its future-compatibility properties. In this particular case, I don't see that as strongly beneficial. As a bonus, if we do drop `include` as a supported parameter, the `TypeError` people will get if they upgrade the SDK communicates that unambiguously, whereas a service error might not.

I'm aware that its future is up for debate, but
- there is no timeline for its removal
- it's on our site as a documented feature
- even if it is removed, that will be a slow process with lots of prior warning
- it is arguably one of the most important things that Auth can do

I'm gonna echo what I wrote [here](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/462#issuecomment-379673580).

In the light of this issue I think it would be helpful if we try to follow the habit that all new code lands with type hints for function signatures right way.

I know there's a bit of controversy around the question whether trivial code needs type hints or not and we haven't set an official rule about this but my *personal* view is that all function signatures should be typed (with the exception of those that are explicitly ignored via special comments because they are too dynamic to type out).

In the long run this would allow us to enable stricter mypy checks (`--disallow-untyped-defs`) that will reject untyped function signatures to land in the code base leaving us with the benefits of a code base that is easier to explore/navigate, enables better IDE support as well as eliminates a whole class of easy to catch bugs.

If we could reach consensus across the team about this we could write it down in the contributing guidelines so that we can refer to that when reviewing PRs.

Docker seemed to have changed the way their tags work.
Your image got created checkout the relationship between your running container and the image:
docker image ls
docker container ps
There will be atleast one image id that matched to your runnning container.
You can now push the image <to docker repo>following these steps:
1) Create a tag for the image that you want to push:
Eg:
docker image tag af0b2985d9a8 friendlyhello
docker image tag <image id> <tagname>

2) login to the docker repo:
docker login

3) Create tag for your locally (tested) image in the docker repo:
eg: docker tag friendlyhello divyanks:py-evm
docker tag local_tag <repo>

4) Push the locally tagged repo 
Eg:
docker push divyanks:py-evm
docker push <tag name created in step 3>

I'm curious to hear other people's ideas on how to implement this.  Here is the best I've thought of thus far.

First, we need to modify how web3 does requests to support the concept of *building* a request.  I'm thinking that all of the *callable*s that current issue requests get a new api like the following.

```python
>>> w3.eth.getBalance.build_request('0x1234abcd...')
Request(['eth_getBalance', ['0x1234abcd...']])
```

Then we can do something like this.

```python
>>> w3.batch_request(
...     w3.eth.getBalance('0x1234abcd...'),
...     w3.eth.getCode('0x1234abcd...'),
...     ...
... )
...
(
    80000000000  # result of `getBalance`
    '0x63deadbeef'  # result of `getCode`
    ...
)
```

Updates will also have to happen for how middlewares process requests and responses but I think this will be doable by adding a new `Manager.batch_request_blocking` which handles the different logic.

(pasted from an internal wiki -- you probably don't have to do all of that -- just the main packages from multiple channels)



Notes on building a redistributable version using the Conda distribution of Python...

# Setting up the conda environment

Use the [Miniconda binary installer](http://conda.pydata.org/miniconda.html), then configure the environment and install packages:

```
conda config --add channels conda-forge
conda config --add channels NOAA-ORR-ERD
conda install conda-build
```
# Installing packages
Install the source code if you have not already:
```
git clone git@gitlab.orr.noaa.gov:erd/MapRoom.git
cd maproom
```
Install the required packages:
```
conda env create -f maproom_environment.yml
source activate maproom
```
then install the manual dependencies (pytriangle, post_gnome, my hacked version of OWSLib) using a script in MapRoom:
```
python installdeps.py
```

# Running MapRoom from source

MapRoom needs access to the windowing system, so you have to run it using
```
pythonw maproom.py
```

# Creating the application bundle

Pyinstaller has a few quirks, so I created a build script to work around the issues. At the top level directory, use the script:
```
./build_pyinstaller.sh
```
and it should create a file ``dist-[VERSION]/Maproom.app`` that can be opened and an archive file ``dist-[VERSION]/Maproom-[VERSION]-darwin.tbz`` that can be transported to another machine.

> Why does conda check only `${CONDA_PREFIX}/info/index.json` for determining whether or not it can use `LINK_HARD` for the entire package? In other words, how/why is this file the source of truth?

I've started making fairly extensive use of "magic files" (see https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.4.x/conda/base/constants.py#L108-L110).  (Hmmm even the name sort of goes a long with the theme here.)  `info/index.json` should be added to the list of constants.

The value of this pattern really solidified somewhere around conda 4.3.13, and it's really proven itself since.  We can make assumptions that these files are indicative of the state of the "unit" they represent, and we will be right the VERY VAST majority of the time.  It's only under extremely exceptional circumstances that we'll be wrong (like if a user `chmod`s a single random file for some reason somewhere), and we can then balk without too much consequence if/when that ever happens (due in large part to how rock solid the unlink-link transaction has been).

For the three magic files I linked to above, the test we're throwing at them is "are you--and the unit you represent--writable or read-only?"  Before this magic file change, conda would test permissions by just seeing if a file of a random name could be created (and then removed) in a directory.  Obviously problematic.

So is it really ok to use a single file to represent the state of a whole unit of files?  For the three examples I've been talking about so far, I think so.  If you think through how we'd handle the situation if we tested every single file in the unit of files, and found one to be abnormal compared to the others, I don't think the end result would be much different than what we're already doing now.  It's just that the path from point A to point B--the in between--might be a little different.

Now, to the `info/index.json` file specifically... You use `${CONDA_PREFIX}/info/index.json`, which is actually incorrect.  `info` directories from conda packages are left out of what is linked into a conda prefix.  More accurate is `<EXTRACTED_CONDA_PACKAGE_DIRECTORY>/info/index.json`.  We use `info/index.json` because that is absolutely guaranteed to be there for every conda package.  If a tarball does not have an `info/index.json` file, then it is not a conda package.  Easy test.  Here again for the hard-link test, I think it's safe to use this one path as a proxy for the state of the entire package.  If you think of the general flow, we download tarballs to a cache directory, and then extract them there too.  They're extracted in one go as a single unit.  Under any normal circumstance it doesn't make sense that, for example, that the package could be extracted on more than one mount point.  Similarly, on the other end, we're making the assumption that a conda prefix is all contained within a single mount point (this is probably the more tenuous assumption).

What happens if we're wrong and the file can't be hard-linked?  First, we use a procedure to figure out what the *requested* link type is when linking a package into a prefix.  https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.3.17/conda/core/link.py#L48-L58  That function should really be named `determine_requested_link_type()`; the result is called [`requested_link_type`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.3.17/conda/core/link.py#L401) in many other places in the code, and that's more accurate.  It's a requested link type because it's not honored in all situations.  There a few places of logic [here](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.3.17/conda/core/path_actions.py#L156-L195) and [here](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.3.17/conda/gateways/disk/create.py#L217-L254) where we'll override the request saying no, you can't have that.

Are there edge cases?  Probably still a few.  Much of this strategy conda has been using well before I started working on the code base.  We haven't yet encountered an edge case that presents a fatal flaw in the design.

> Is there an option in conda using which I can specify that I want to prefer soft-links over hard-links, while creating new environments (using conda create -n or conda env create ...), regardless of whether creating hard-links is possible? Why/Why not?

The short logic in [`determine_link_type()`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.3.17/conda/core/link.py#L48-L58) shows the available configuration parameters and how they're handled.  To your question directly, the answer is 'yes', and the configuration parameter is `always_softlink: yes` in a condarc file and `CONDA_ALWAYS_SOFTLINK=yes` as an environment variable.  In this case, the value `always_copy` has to be `False` (which is the default) for this to work.  These are boolean switches rather than a single enum parameter because, well, probably for the same reason conda 'features' are right now binary on-off rather than enums.  No further comments on design philosophy limitations there.  The `always_softlink` option was actually added somewhat recently.  The 'why' discussion is at https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/3308.

@tete1030 is right, this isn't completely fixed. As seen in the traceback, the problem is with the `print(some_potential_unicode_string)` call. Python 2 sets the encoding of `stdout`/`stderr` to `None` if not run in a terminal context and thus falls back to ASCII.
@tete1030: As a workaround you can do one of the following:
- use Python 3 in the `base` environment (which I always recommend),
- run `PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 source activate` instead of just `source activate`,
- run `export PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8` once per shell session.

----

@kalefranz: There are a couple of options we have to handle this:
1. In https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.4.8/conda/activate.py#L132
```diff
-            return self.command_join.join(commands)
+            output = self.command_join.join(commands)
+            if PY2:
+                return ensure_binary(output)
+            return output
```
2. Somewhere prior to the `print` call (maybe early on, e.g., in `cli.main`?) do something like:
```python
from codecs import getwriter
from locale import getpreferredencoding
import sys


for stream_name in ("stdout", "stderr"):
    stream = getattr(sys, stream_name)
    if stream.encoding:
        continue
    encoding = getpreferredencoding()
    try:
        encoder = getwriter(encoding)
    except LookupError:
        encoder = getwriter("UTF-8")
    base_stream = getattr(stream, "buffer", stream)
    setattr(sys, stream_name, encoder(base_stream))
```
3. In https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.4.8/conda/shell/etc/profile.d/conda.sh#L55 (and others)
```diff
-    ask_conda="$(PS1="$PS1" $_CONDA_EXE shell.posix activate "$@")" || return $?
+    ask_conda="$(PS1="$PS1" PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 $_CONDA_EXE shell.posix activate "$@")" || return $?
```

I would definitely avoid 3. and rather do it in Python code do avoid duplicate and heterogeneous code.
So, IMO, the viable options are:
1. Do only a quick fix, being very precise and only handle Unicode output for this single `print` call.
2. Apply a much wider fix that isn't surgical at all, but would help with other potential Unicode output on `stdout` and `stderr` when `conda` is used by other programs/scripts/shell functions.

We've had so many problems with pyopenssl and cryptography lately.  It's unfortunate.

Regarding the test that was added, there might be a misunderstanding on the semantic of the significant figure argument when creating a histogram that may explain why your test fails. The significant figure will tell HdrHistogram the required precision of the each value recorded across the allowed range. For example a precision of 2, means the histogram will alway keep recorded values within 0.01% of its actual value. For example, if the range is 1 usec to 100 seconds, the recorded value at the 1 usec level will be +/- 0.01usec and at the 100 second level will be +/- 1 sec.
This is directly derived from the way buckets are being carved for counting intervals. On a simple histogram, buckets are linear (meaning each bucket represents a fixed interval). This obviously does not scale well for large ranges and small precision. HdrHistogram implements non linear buckets where each interval is dependent on the precision level: 1 usec level will have intervals of 0.01 usec while 1 sec level will have intervals of 1 sec. This helps in cutting down the number of buckets while preserving a relative precision at every interval level - hence the name High Dynamic Range histogram. This stems from the observation that if you care about a high dynamic range (say 1 usec to 1 day) in general you want to be very accurate at the low range but it does not make usually sense to be accurate to the sub usec level when your numbers go as high a multiple hours (because at that level, you;d have much bigger fish to fry to chase sub usec values).

The biggest challenge with maintaining Arch Linux package for `azure-cli` is the amount of dependencies to keep track of (they all should be published as separate packages, e.g. now I marked [python-adal](https://aur.archlinux.org/packages/python-adal/) as outdated), and at the same time there are no packages yet for modules within [azure-sdk](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python) (currently only [python-azure-git](https://aur.archlinux.org/packages/python-azure-git) exists, which is a hack, it clones the entire repo and installs everything from master branch.

Currently the Arch Linux packages are all being maintained by @optlink ([their account](https://aur.archlinux.org/account/optlink) contains email address for communication). If you are considering to provide official support, I've seen other companies just adopting the existing AUR packages for their products (basically getting write permissions), and I recommend doing the same — I'm sure @optlink will be happy to assist. That way, you are only left with just bumping the versions in time (package + dependencies), which would be a great help to the community (and for bonus points, if you split `python-azure-git` into multiple packages that also follow official releases instead of `master` branch, that would be even more awesome).

I can also help if you need anything.

The sparse file upload optimization for page blobs is contained in the following 2 commits: [commit1](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/commit/eb9183a20ba67cdbab868df3241258e93eb3cc3f) and [commit2](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/commit/ff4e72d20e901d0ae9e011328c0bae137d309f7e). In short, we read each chunk before uploading it to verify that it is not empty. We do store the file sparsely on the server side, but while uploading, reading the source file is still required. Thus, it might be almost impossible that uploading the sparse file takes the same amount of time as uploading only the non-sparse parts, because reading the source file could be a bottleneck. A fairer comparison would be uploading the sparse file with and without this optimization(current CLI vs previous CLI before optimization). 

You can verify that the file is stored sparsely by performing a [get page range](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/commit/eb9183a20ba67cdbab868df3241258e93eb3cc3f#diff-e720b419113af430e8edd2cd7551da73R654).

We have debated on this for several times. I have always wanted to display the ip-address in `vm show`.  The main augment was it might slow things down. 
But for `vm show` it only involves 2 extra rest calls which are to get `nic` and then the `public-ip`.  The extra cost won't be that significant, as it mostly gets amortized in command loading, parsing and output.

When you get a 409 copy error because the target already has an async copy pending, you should get ‘PendingCopyOperation’ in the error information body to indicate the reason for the failure in addition to the standard 409 Conflict. This body should tell you why it failed.

While sync/async can’t be predicted with cross account copy, note that within an account copies will always be sync. Also, when start copy is initially called you will get a ‘x-ms-copy-status’ return header set to either Pending or Success. If it is Pending, you know the copy is async and if you’d like, can abort it.

So, if you’d like to abort async copies you should be able to do that immediately after the start (using x-ms-copy-status) or using the error body when the Conflict occurs.

```python
import json
import asyncio
import logging
from aiohttp import web
from functools import wraps

from pymongo import MongoClient
import os
# Mongo setup:
client = MongoClient(os.getenv('MongoDBURL'))
db = client['logs']

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
log = logging.getLogger()


def rest_handler(handler_func):
    """
    Allows handlers to return dict.
    Errors are handled and put in json format.
    """
    @wraps(handler_func)
    def wrapper(self, request):
        error_code = None
        try:
            res = yield from handler_func(self, request)
            result = dict(status='OK', result=res)
        except web.HTTPClientError as e:
            log.warning('Http error: %r %r', e.status_code, e.reason,
                        exc_info=True)
            error_code = e.status_code
            result = dict(error_code=error_code,
                          error_reason=e.reason,
                          status='FAILED')
        except Exception as e:
            log.warning('Server error', exc_info=True)
            error_code = 500
            result = dict(error_code=error_code,
                          error_reason='Unhandled exception',
                          status='FAILED')

        assert isinstance(result, dict)
        body = json.dumps(result).encode('utf-8')
        result = web.Response(body=body)
        result.headers['Content-Type'] = 'application/json'
        if error_code:
            result.set_status(error_code)
        return result

    return wrapper

class Rest:

    @asyncio.coroutine
    def index(self, request):
        return web.Response(body=b"Hello, world")

    @rest_handler
    @asyncio.coroutine
    def log(self, request):

        data = yield from request.post()


        # Get the Log Type to write to proper Mongo Collection / DB

        collection = db[dict(data)['col_name']]
        data_inserted = json.loads(json.dumps(dict(data)))
        # Data Inserted to Database here:
        response = collection.insert_one(data_inserted)

        # print(response.inserted_id)
        log.info('Message reveived %r', data_inserted)
        return ""


# def main():
rest = Rest()

app = web.Application()
app.router.add_route('GET', '/', rest.index)
app.router.add_route('POST', '/log', rest.log)

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
handler = app.make_handler()
f = loop.create_server(handler, '0.0.0.0', 3000)
srv = loop.run_until_complete(f)
log.info('serving on %r', srv.sockets[0].getsockname())
try:
    loop.run_forever()
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    log.info('\nBye')
    loop.run_until_complete(handler.finish_connections(1.0))
    srv.close()
    loop.run_until_complete(srv.wait_closed())
    loop.run_until_complete(app.finish())
    loop.close()
```

> I view the immediate submission of requests as a very important feature. We mustn't wait for the previous execution to complete before sending the next request, so chaining futures in Run All doesn't sound good to me. It also seems like a violation that code in a subsequent cell requires frontend interaction after the result of a previous cell.

I would agree with you completely if we were just talking about notebooks where all cells are eval/response type. Meaning, once the response reaches the notebook, that cell is really done.

HTML and JS magics, the COMM channel and Widgets introduce the likelihood of that more has to be executed before a cell could be consider fully done. 

Consider ipywidgets as another example.  I don't have concrete proof, but I think that it currently by-passes this issue all-together because it preloads all its widgets JS code ahead of time on notebook page load. If ipywidgets did not do this, there would be no guarantees that the widgets would be available for next cell's result due to async AMD loads. Since it is a close-set of widgets, it is able to implement the work-around, although it does so by impacting load times of all notebook pages regardless if they have widgets or not (for the record, declarativewidgets does the same).

Belief me that I understand that this is not a simple issue. Changing the way cells are executed is changing the core of the notebook. There is also the question of how do you really know when a cell is really done. Even harder if the cell involves async loading of JS code. 

I think your suggestion of making Cell.execute return a promise and allow overriding might be a start, but it also is punting on the issue. I personally don't have a good solution to offer either, but do think that more discussion should take place.

Hey @MikiSoft--
Sorry for a delayed response  -- this answer is probably months too late, but I didn't have a chance to do much maintenance work on this package.  Hopefully there will be something helpful here.  

The problem with `None` results in Python 3 is that there's no real generalized way to handle them -- this [StackOverflow answer](http://stackoverflow.com/a/12971697/3362468) gives a pretty good explanation of the problem.  The solution provided can't really be generalized, though -- if you return an empty string instead of `None`, that will cause the same type error if a user is sorting a numeric field with None (rather than a string-based field with `None`).  

However, the MultipleModelAPIView allows the user to override the `queryList_sort` function to implement custom sorting.  Here's a simplified example that would address the `None` issue -- you'd probably need to tailor it more (for example: I didn't replicate the sort_descending functionality of the original sorting function -- you can look at the MultipleModeMixin source to figure out how to implement that if you need to):

```
class CustomSortingView(MultipleModelAPIView):
    queryList = (
        ...
    )
    flat = True
    sorting_field = 'my_field_with_nulls'

    def queryList_sort(self, results):
          field = self.sorting_field
          return sorted(results, key=lambda x: x[field] if x[field] else "")
```

There's no new functionality. We're just replacing `make_tuple` (which we use often) with `tuple`. Maybe users will expect more from something called `list` than something called `make_list`. But the situation is the same with autograd.numpy - assignment to arrays breaks the Autograd illusion.

This may be true. I've never tried to work around Python ssl to invoke features in the underlying openssl backend. However, I would **never** advocate for this approach. The use of unsupported or undocumented security features is a danger zone and I do not want to encourage PyKMIP users to enter it.

If a user is able to install or update openssl to support TLS1.2, that user should also be able to update Python to 2.7.9+ or 3.4+. Python 2.7.9 was released in December 2014 and Python 3.4 was released in March 2014. If a user is tied to an old version of Python due to backwards compatibility issues, then that user needs to make a tradeoff decision between supporting old features and better security.

I would rather not have to manage the product_class foreign key on both the parent and child, because that introduces the possibility of the parent and child having different product classes, which doesn't really make sense to me.

For instance, if the parent and children have product_class_id == T-Shirt, and then the parent is updated so that product_class == Sweater, then we have to make sure to go and update the product_class for all of the children to be Sweater. If we force children to have a null value, then we don't need to go out of our way to ensure that they match up.

(To use more formal language, having the foreign key relationship be on both models is less normalized than simply having it on the parent.)

Are you running with the Apache plugin?

Running the `certonly` subcommand will get you a cert but not install it.

However the symlinks below `/etc/letsencrypt/live` will always be updated by that command to point to the newest cert, so if you have those entries in your conf files, any webserver reload event will cause them to be deployed.

The code in `renewer.py`, which isn't ready for use yet, actually has the ability to wait for a defined number of days before updating those symlinks.  You can emulate its behaviour by having your conf file point to the entries in `/etc/letsencrypt/archive/`, and updating them manually when you're ready to deploy a new cert.

This has been resolved. `letsencrypt-auto` still checks for updates on every run, however, it now does so with a single GET request. When this issue was written, `letsencrypt-auto` would check for updates for all of our Python dependencies which really slowed down the script.

Furthermore, if you don't want it to check for updates at all, you can now provide the `--no-self-upgrade` flag which will skip this step entirely.

The reason libffi is not listed as an installation requirement is because libffi, while required, ships with OS X. In this particular case you almost certainly ran into a bug with the cffi 1.4.1 wheels shipped on PyPI that were improperly linked against homebrew libffi rather than the system libffi. When this bug was discovered the wheels were deleted, but any user with the cached wheel would continue to have the problem. This was subsequently resolved with the release of cffi 1.4.2. This issue was an unfortunate confluence of several complex interactions and was not the fault of lets encrypt in any way.

This is fairly important functionality and I'd like it available in old versions of dateutil as well, so I have decided that the way forward here is to:

1. Release a `dateutil.tzdata` package that provides the `zoneinfo` files (we could also call this `zoneinfodata`).
2. Release a third package, `dateutil.future_compat` (I'm also open to name suggestions for this), which patches sufficiently old versions of `dateutil` with the updated `zoneinfo` functionality.
3. Switch over all future versions of `dateutil` to use `dateutil.tzdata` or whatever it's called by default.

This is by analogy to the `__future__` imports in CPython.

I'm thinking it would look something like this:

```python
from dateutil.future_compat import tzdata
```

And importing `tzdata` will have the side effect of patching `dateutil.zoneinfo` such that it first attempts to import the `tzdata` from `dateutil.tzdata` and otherwise falls back to the included data.

In version 3.0, `dateutil.tzdata` will be a hard dependency for `dateutil` and there will be no separately packaged data.

So this turned into a monster PR... my apologies

I decided to make a small module (`util/redux`) to hold various redux utilities:

`ResourceActions` - a class that hooks up the basic CRUD operations for a resource
`Reducer` - a blueprint class for building reducers, for example:

```js
const initialState = { message: '' }

let messageReducer = new Reducer(initialState)

messageReducer.handle('UPDATE_MESSAGE', (state, action) => R.merge(state, { message: action.message }))

// Get the actual reducer function
export messageReducer.getReducer()
```

`buildManyResourceReducer` - generates a reducer with hooks for a resource listing
`buildSingleResourceReducer` - generates a reducer with hooks for a single resource

`pending`, `fulfilled`, `rejected` - helper functions for async actions

Let me know what you guys think of this approach! I'd like to document all of it in the wiki, but before that happens I want to make sure it's logical. I find that it not only reduces boilerplate but also makes the code easier to read/understand, mainly because those reducer switch statements could get pretty ugly.

**Data types**: 

Loom has so far been very restricted in the data types allowed for attributes. The spec says `float64` and `string` (fixed-length ASCII) only. This is to maximize compatibility. 

For strings, I propose to stick to ascii, but to use XML entity encoding (e.g. things like `ä`). This allows you to pass Unicode through HDF5 while retaining compatibility with MATLAB and other languages that don't speak Unicode. It means that you will need to do the equivalent of `html.unescape(x)` for each string upon reading.

For numbers, I propose to be more generous, and allow 8, 16, 32 and 64-bit ints, both signed and unsigned, as well as both 32 and 64-bit floats. I propose these same types would be allowed for the matrix layers (but no strings).

We store bools as numbers (1 and 0) currently, and I propose they would be unsigned bytes. This works out of the box with both MATLAB and C/C++.

1. One interesting characteristic of WARP is that it is effectively playing against itself: it is preferentially using examples that it scores highly as negative examples. Up to a certain point, this allows it to express user preferences better; beyond that point, it is self-defeating, as it is selecting the examples that it should recommend and treating them as negatives. The extent to which that happens is governed by the `max_sampled` [hyperparameter](http://lyst.github.io/lightfm/docs/lightfm.html#lightfm.LightFM).

I suspect that this is what happening in your case. Instead of switching to BPR, can you try decreasing that hyperparameter? Otherwise, I don't see any obvious problems with switching losses. It's interesting that you find different results with adagrad and adadelta, I haven't observed that.

2. `sample_weight` is the right way of using this in the LightFM model. If you are optimizing for some weighted objective function, changing the weights here will probably help in achieving your goal.

3. I'm open to a submodule along the lines of `lightfm.contrib` which would contain high-quality, but not strictly speaking supported functionality (say, with optional dependencies). I'd be happy to discuss your additions (but I have to warn I can be quite picky!). Let me first try to state my current thinking about how to structure unit tests in pvlib in the context of this pull request.

1. The tests in ``test_atmosphere.py`` should ensure that the ``atmosphere.first_solar_spectral_correction`` function returns the desired output for a variety of function inputs. The tests should be independent of other pvlib functions (see #394). The tests should ensure that all reasonable combinations of input types (floats, nans, arrays, series, scalars, etc) work as expected. I think the existing tests for this function are reasonably good and no new work is needed in this pull request.

2. The tests in ``test_pvsystem.py`` should ensure that the ``PVSystem.first_solar_spectral_correction`` method calls the ``atmosphere.first_solar_spectral_correction`` function with the desired inputs determined from all relevant configurations of a ``PVSystem`` object. A test should also ensure that the method does return some reasonable data, though the precise values of the data should be covered by tests in item 1.

3. The tests in ``test_modelchain.py`` should ensure that ``ModelChain.__init__`` correctly configures the ``ModelChain`` object to eventually run ``PVSystem.first_solar_spectral_correction``. A test should ensure that method is eventually run in the course of ``Model_chain.run_model``. A test should ensure that the model selection does have a reasonable effect on the subsequent calculations, though the precise values of the data should be covered by tests in item 1.

That thinking has evolved from the many mistakes that I have made in writing pvlib tests, too many of which are still with us.

The function-level tests in item 1 are relatively straight forward so long as we avoid the dependency issue @cwhanse pointed out in #394. I used to be bad and lazy about that, so there are some older tests that have dependencies that they shouldn't have.

The ``PVSystem`` and ``ModelChain`` tests are less straight forward to implement. I often thought that I could reasonably check items 2 and 3 by testing that a subset of user-level numeric inputs produced the desired numeric outputs. Of course, these higher-level objects effectively inject a bunch of dependencies into the tests, and we're back at issue #394. The plus side is that they are simple to write the first time.

Moving forward to comments from @cwhanse and @adriesse 

> I recalculate the expected values by running each model in the chain. I don't like this approach, because a future change to one of the upstream functions could cause this test of the spectral models to fail.

Agreed. 

> Change the test pattern to not use a ModelChain object. This is a problem because it wouldn't use the ModelChain methods which assign the spectral model method to the ModelChain object.

We will want tests that only use a ``PVSystem`` object (item 2) and tests that do use a ``ModelChain`` object (item 3). 

> Add code to ModelChain.__init__ to handle airmass as a kwarg, and impose user-supplied values in place of the calculation using times and location. This is my least liked option because I don't see a use of the additional code beyond this made-up test.

Agreed -- I think we can come up with a better way.

> Perhaps it is not necessary to check the precise values that are returned using the ModelChain. If the spectral calculation function is already verified to be correct through its own tests, then you really just need to test/exercise the additional code related to the ModelChain integration. 

Agreed, though I do want to emphasize the importance (in my mind) of separating tests at the level of ``PVSystem`` (item 2) and ``ModelChain`` (item 3). I don't know how to write this kind of test, though, and welcome suggestions. I know it's possible to assert that a function is called (maybe even with specific arguments), but I don't have practice doing it. I think pytest has some support for this kind of testing. The ``mock`` library is also useful for related testing problems.

> I suppose the inference code would need to be tested somehow too. That might be easier if the result of any inferences were explicitly stored in the ModelChain, so a test (or a user) could check what happened.

The inferences are stored in the sense that a specific model's corresponding method e.g. ``ModelChain.first_solar_spectral_loss`` is assigned to the corresponding generic attribute e.g. ``ModelChain._spectral_model``. A test could ensure that this happens given some input ``PVSystem`` configurations. Also, the string representation of a ``ModelChain`` allows a user to easily check what happened with the inference using ``print(my_model_object)``. 

How to move forward? I will start by learning more about pytest and mock in an effort to design test templates that let us address items 2 and 3 while avoiding unintended dependencies. I'll look into that this week, though it may take longer to come up with a good solution. I am also ok moving forward with something like @cwhanse's option 1, but it's complicated by the fact that some of the existing model chain tests break due to the corrections applied by this new feature. 

Finally, we should add some text and examples to the contributing section of the documentation if we can come to some agreement about testing principles and practice.

On a MiSeq, index metrics is written out by RTA.
On a MiniSeq, index metrics is written out when you run bcl2fastq (or the secondary analysis pipe line ISAS).

As for the By Cycle Plot, the MiSeq is a 4 channel system, where each channel is labeled with a base. So, you get 4 intensity curves labeled by each base.

The MiniSeq is a 2 channel system, where each channel is labeled by the color of the laser. So, you get 2 intensity curves labeled by the color.

The bases are actually derived in a different manner, you can see this document below (Figure 2), for more information.

http://www.illumina.com/content/dam/illumina-marketing/documents/products/techspotlights/techspotlight_two-channel_sbs.pdf

> Much better and still not overfitting, but not solved according to the 0.04 absolute error metric.

> Maybe we should implement that metric as well and adjust the hyperparameters.

Hmm, the example does not exactly follow any of these existing variants:
* [Hochreiter et al 1997](http://deeplearning.cs.cmu.edu/pdfs/Hochreiter97_lstm.pdf), Sect. 5.4
* [Martens et al 2011](http://www.cs.utoronto.ca/~ilya/pubs/2011/HF-RNN_supp.pdf), Sect. 2.1
* [Sutskever et al 2013](www.cs.toronto.edu/~fritz/absps/momentum.pdf), Sect. A.4

Hochreiter used uniform samples from (-1, 1), marked the first and last time step with -1, sampled the second marked item within (0, T/2 - 1), and set the target to 0.5 + (x1 + x2) / 4.
Martens used uniform samples from (0, 1) and marked items as we do, but sampled the second marked item within (T/10, T/2), and set the target to (x1 + x2) / 2.
Sutskever is not very clear, they both say that the target is (x1 + x2) and that they follow Martens.
Both Hochreiter and Martens used the < 0.04 metric, which are compatible (the different input scaling is countered by the different target scaling).

I'm inclined to follow Martens, but set the target to (x1 + x2) and evaluate with < 0.08. If we set the target to (x1 + x2) / 2, people will be confused again.

Sorry for the delay, it's probably not relevant any more now, but for that use case I wouldn't suggest slicing and doing multiple convolutions, but reshaping the tensor from (batchsize, channels, rows, cols) to (batchsize*channels, 1, rows, cols), then do a normal 2d convolution. This will use the same set of weights for all channels, since they're interpreted as different examples in a batch now. In the end, reshape back to (batchsize, ...) to restore the original grouping (note that if you want to do multiple such convolutions and only pool in between, you can keep the (batchsize*channels, ...) layout until the very end).

I agree with @oruebel on this issue... I appreciate the enthusiasm to charge ahead, but this decision has major implications for development, so we really need to take the time to flush out the options.

Here are my thoughts on the issues that have been raised thus far:

1. As simple as writing 2/3 compatible code might seem, the minimal up-front costs comes with a significant trade-off. Writing cross-compatible code limits us to only features of Python 3 that have an equivalent in Python 2. Given that Python 3.x is the future, we need to make writing a codebase that can evolve with the progression of 3.x a priority. To be more specific, there are many features of 3.x that could be useful, but we are not able to use because they have no equivalent in Python 2. The PyNWB codebase already uses one of these i.e. \_\_prepare\_\_. By keeping a separate Python 2 fork for releases, we can actively develop in Python 3.x and deal with 2 vs. 3 differences at release time. 

2. Writing cross-compatible code has one more subtle, yet significant implication. Strings are unicode in Python 3, and ASCII in Python 2. This means that the 2/3 compatible code needs ASCII when run in Python 2, but can use unicode when run in Python 3. So, our codebase would behave drastically different when run in different version of Python. An example of this is when h5py writes strings--the same line of code (`h5py.special_dtype(vlen=str)`), writes UTF-8 in Python 3, but writes ASCII in Python 2.  

3. The issue of a rapidly changing code base is really the result of early development. There has yet to be a stable release--we are really still in alpha. Upon reaching a stable release, I do not see PyNWB being a rapidly changing the code base in the long-term.

OK, we worked it out. 

This can occur when either of the two cases happen:

1. you don't wait long enough for the `npm run build:desktop:watch` to finish before running `npm run spawn`

2. If you are running `npm run start` in a third terminal at the same time as `npm run build:desktop:watch`

So the solution is to make sure that `npm run build:desktop:watch` finishes before running `npm run spawn`. My guess is the readme should reflect that condition.

Manual calculation of the distances for the same location ([0, 0]) of the target area.

```python

import numpy as np
from pyresample import geometry
from pyresample import _spatial_mp

# Converter from lonlats to cartesian coordinates
cartesian = _spatial_mp.Cartesian()

# Source data coordinates
lons, lats = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-25., 40., num=100), np.linspace(45., 75., num=100))
# Source coordinates in cartesian space
input_coords = cartesian.transform_lonlats(lons.ravel(), lats.ravel())

# Target area definition
target_def = geometry.AreaDefinition('areaD', 'Europe (3km, HRV, VTC)',
                                     'areaD',
                                     {'a': '6378144.0', 'b': '6356759.0',
                                      'lat_0': '50.00', 'lat_ts': '50.00',
                                      'lon_0': '8.00', 'proj': 'stere'},
                                     4, 4,
                                     [-1370912.72, -909968.64000000001,
                                      1029087.28, 1490031.3600000001])
# Target coordinates
t_lons, t_lats = target_def.get_lonlats()
# Target coordinates in cartesian space
output_coords = cartesian.transform_lonlats(t_lons.ravel(), t_lats.ravel())

# Calculate distance for the north-western corner point
a_d = (input_coords[:, 0] - output_coords[0, 0])**2
b_d = (input_coords[:, 1] - output_coords[0, 1])**2
c_d = (input_coords[:, 2] - output_coords[0, 2])**2
dists = np.sqrt(a_d + b_d + c_d)

# Print the 6 shortest distances
print(np.sort(dists)[:6])
```

> I'm increasingly leaning toward a helper function that takes a couple non-percent-encoded arguments and gives back a percent-encoded userinfo string suitable to passing to URL methods.

I still disagree with this pretty strongly, because you have this problem over and over again - in the userinfo, in the path, in the query, in the fragment - and in every place you need slightly different versions of the helper function.

I also disagree because it's not obvious to users of the URL object that *usernames can't contain colons*, and making the user do escaping themselves is abdicating responsibility for the whole point of the library.

So I think we need to:

1. separate "username" and "password" and have restrictions on both of them, particularly that "username" can't contain ":".
2. add some kind of consistent idiom for addressing "fully decoded" elements of a URL, including username, password, path, query, and fragment; *maybe* "host" should be in there as well, even though hostnames don't have any escape equivalent (you can't escape a colon or a slash in a hostname, they just can't contain them).

```js        
        {
            "bucket_id": "bid",
            "id": "bid",
            "permissions": [
                "write",
                "read",
                "collection:create",
                "group:create"
            ],
            "resource_name": "bucket",
            "uri": "/buckets/bid"
        }
```
So we can see that we have `"uri": "/buckets/bid"` and the perm `collection:create`.

The current behaviour is to only calculate the approximation coefficient at the highest level using `wavedec`. I think the wavelet packet module allows you to pick an arbitrary coefficient & level. I have not used it much in my work so I don't really know the details of it. I think this is correct:

```
wp = pywt.WaveletPacket(data, 'haar')
coeffs = [wp['a' * level].data for level in range(1, 9)]
```

If you do not want to use that, then using `dwt` on the previous scaling coefficient in your loop would be more efficient, since your method starts from the base level on each loop iteration:

```
data = [2] * 256
coeffs = [pywt.dwt(data, w, mode='per')[0]]
for i in range(1, 8):
  coeffs.append(pywt.dwt(coeffs[-1], w, mode='per')[0])
```

If motion isn't a concern, you should be able to take multiple video frames with a "full" exposure (33.33ms at 30fps) and add/average them together. The only downside are possible motion artifacts in fast moving images. But it should work for a static scene.

I boot the Ubuntu in Aero kernel, and here are the step-by-step instructions to make D435 work on Aero Compute Board. You might need to disconnect and reconnect DS435 several times if it's not recognized.

1.	Install aero- system on the Ubuntu customer already installed:
echo 'deb https://download.01.org/aero/deb xenial main' | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/intel-aero.list
wget -qO - https://download.01.org/aero/deb/intel-aero-deb.key | sudo apt-key add -
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get upgrade
sudo apt-get -y install gstreamer-1.0 libgstreamer-plugins-base1.0-dev libgstrtspserver-1.0-dev gstreamer1.0-vaapi gstreamer1.0-plugins-base gstreamer1.0-plugins-good gstreamer1.0-plugins-bad gstreamer1.0-libav ffmpeg v4l-utils
sudo apt-get -y install aero-system
sudo reboot 
2.	Get aero version and update BIOS
       sudo aero-get-version.py
       sudo aero-bios-update
       sudo reboot (Use advanced option is necessary to boot to aero kernel. ‘uname -r’ should show “4.4.76-Aero-1.2” after reboot.)
3.	Update FPGA
       sudo jam -aprogram /etc/fpga/aero-compute-board.jam
4.	Update system
       sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade && sudo apt-get dist-upgrade
       sudo apt-get install --install-recommends linux-generic-lts-xenial xserver-xorg-core-lts-xenial xserver-xorg-lts-xenial xserver-xorg-video-all-lts-xenial xserver-xorg-input-all-lts-xenial libwayland-egl1-mesa-lts-xenial
       sudo update-grub && sudo reboot
5.	Install packages needed for librealsense
        sudo apt-get install git cmake
               sudo apt-get install libusb-1.0-0-dev pkg-config libgtk-3-dev
                sudo apt-get install libglfw3-dev
6.	Get librealsense source code and build it:
         git clone https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense.git (Pick a folder for the code).
          Navigate to librealsense root directory and run mkdir build && cd build
          cmake ../ -DBUILD_EXAMPLES=true
    sudo make uninstall && make clean && make && sudo make install    
7.	Replace the “patch-realsense-ubuntu-xenial.sh” with the modified version (attached) in the librealsense root directory:
         ./scripts/patch-realsense-ubuntu-xenial.sh
         Connect the camera and Reboot
8.	Try if DS5 is recognized by Aero Compute Board:
          ls /dev/video* should show /dev/video0, /dev/video1, …,  /dev/video12
          rs_enumerate-devices should show DS5 device
          rsrealsense-viewer should see depth map
9.	Here are the web sites for reference:
a.	https://github.com/intel-aero/meta-intel-aero/wiki/90-(References)-OS-user-Installation
b.	https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/doc/installation.md

To add new replacement functions, you follow this process:

* Create a new folder titled `src/missing/func`, using `src/missing/strlcpy` as a starting point.
* Implement the replacement function and header.
* Update `src/missing/Makefile.am` to reference the new missing function implementation subdirectory.
* Add a new `CHECK_MISSING_FUNC([func])` line to `configure.ac`
* Add `src/missing/func/Makefile` to the `AC_CONFIG_FILES` line in `configure.ac`.

And that should be it.

On Visual Studio, the file `src/missing/func/func.h` would need to be automatically included in all files, and `src/missing/func/func.c` added to the project.
